option_b: Yes, because the landscaper could have obtained possession of the truck through legal action rather than by agreeing to the increased payment.
option_c: No, because the mechanic exploited the landscaper's immediate need for the truck.
option_d: No, because the landscaper had no reasonable alternative but to yield to the mechanic's wrongful threat.
option_e: Yes, because the final price is equivalent to the customary charge for such work.
option_f: No, because the original contract did not include the additional $2,000 payment.
option_g: Yes, because the landscaper agreed in writing to pay the additional cost.
option_h: Yes, because the mechanic completed the repairs as agreed.
option_i: Yes, because the modified contract price did not exceed a reasonable price.
option_j: No, because the mechanic exerted undue influence over the landscaper with respect to the modification.

question_text: A defendant is on trial for robbery. The defendant's attorney plans to call a witness. Nine years earlier, the witness had been convicted of misdemeanor battery. The defendant's attorney has filed a motion disallowing the prosecution from questioning the witness regarding this prior conviction. The motion will likely be
option_a: denied, because the conviction is relevant to the witness's credibility
option_b: denied, because the credibility of a witness may be attacked by any party.
option_c: granted, because the prejudice to the defendant substantially outweighs the probative value of admitting the witness's conviction.
option_d: denied, because the conviction is a matter of public record
option_e: granted, as a matter of right.
option_f: denied, because the defense attorney did not file the motion in a timely manner
option_g: denied, because a period of less than 10 years has elapsed since the date of conviction.
option_h: granted, because the witness's past has no bearing on the defendant's case
option_i: granted, because the conviction is not related to the current case

question_text: Billy was charged with grand theft. The trial began on a Thursday afternoon. The jury was empaneled, sworn and released for the day. Since Friday was the Fourth of July, the judge asked the jurors to return on Monday. The trial began again on Monday morning at 8:30. By late evening the judge had instructed the jury. Due to the lateness of the hour, the jurors were sequestered for the evening to allow them to get an early start the next morning. The jurors returned Tuesday morning and were unable to reach a verdict. Unable to reach a verdict, the trial judge allowed the jurors to go home that evening. On Wednesday morning, the jury assembled and returned a verdict of guilty. On appeal, which of the following is Billy's strongest issue for seeking a reversal?
option_a: The fact that the jury was allowed to go home after being sworn.
option_b: The fact that the jurors did not begin to consider evidence until several days after they were empaneled.
option_c: The fact that the jury was allowed to go home after they began deliberations.
option_d: The fact that the trial began on a holiday weekend.
option_e: The fact that the jury was not given enough time to deliberate.
option_f: The fact that the jury was sequestered for only one night.
option_g: The fact that the jury took several days to return a verdict.
option_h: The fact that the jury returned a verdict of guilty.
option_i: The fact that the jury did not start deliberating until the following week.
option_j: The fact that the trial began late in the afternoon.

question_text: A man is at home in his apartment, alone, late at night. Someone repeatedly buzzes his apartment ringer outside the building, but he doesn't answer it because he is very tired and wants to sleep. Later he hears the door to his apartment being kicked in and he is immediately accosted by a man with a gun, who waves the gun at him and says, "You are getting me your cash or else." The man opens his safe and turns over the money. The perpetrator absconds with the money but is picked up by the police a few minutes later. What common law crimes has the perpetrator probably committed?

Immediately after a shooting incident, the police chased one of the shooters into an apartment where he was apprehended and arrested. While in the apartment, a detective noticed some antique furniture that seemed of collectors' quality and was out of place in the squalidly furnished quarters. He moved and turned over the pieces to look for markings and insignia. He called headquarters, and was told that items with those markings and descriptions were stolen from a museum six months earlier. He seized the items and arrested another occupant, who claimed he owned the items, on charges of burglary. That suspect filed a motion to suppress based on there being a warrantless search without probable cause. The police relied on the plain view doctrine. Will the court likely suppress the evidence?
option_a: Yes, the evidence will be suppressed because the officer did not immediately recognize the items as stolen property.
option_b: No, the evidence was properly seized because the officer had a right to investigate it further once he had properly entered the apartment under exigent circumstances.
option_c: No, the evidence will not be suppressed because the officer had a reasonable suspicion that the items were stolen.
option_d: No, the evidence will not be suppressed because the officer was in a place where he was legally allowed to be when he observed the antique furniture.
option_e: Yes, the evidence must be suppressed because the plain view doctrine does not apply to items that are not immediately identifiable as contraband or evidence of a crime.
option_f: Yes, the evidence will be suppressed because the police had no warrant to search the apartment for additional evidence.
option_g: No, the evidence was subject to further search and seizure because it was in plain view and the officer had a reasonable suspicion that it was contraband.
option_h: Yes, the evidence must be suppressed because the plain view doctrine does not apply where the officer had no probable cause to believe that the observed item was contraband.
option_i: Yes, the evidence will be suppressed because the police can never search a personal residence or seize any unrelated items after entering for exigent circumstances dealing with something else.
option_j: No, the evidence will not be suppressed because the plain view doctrine allows an officer to seize any item that he or she believes is evidence of a crime.

question_text: An owner of a pizza and Italian foods restaurant opens a new location on a street where another owner has a burger restaurant. It seems like business is slow for the pizza place owner and he eyes up his competitor, the burger man, as the reason. The pizza owner starts to make false statements about the burger restaurant and its inferior ingredients, which he says have been causing ptomaine poisoning in some of the customers. When burger joint owner loses customers and business income to pizza place owner, can he sue for conversion?
option_a: No, because conversion consists of appropriating another's real property.
option_b: No, because the burger owner failed to prove that the pizza owner's statements were false.
option_c: No, because there was no interference with his ownership or right of possession to his personal property.
option_d: No, because the pizza owner has the right to advertise his business as he sees fit.
option_e: No, because the burger owner did not suffer any physical harm or damage to his property.
option_f: Yes, conversion is an intentional tort and the pizza owner acted intentionally to convert and did convert part of the burger business.
option_g: Yes, because the pizza owner committed a tort of defamation, which is a form of conversion.
option_h: Yes, because the pizza owner's actions caused economic harm to the burger owner.
option_i: Yes, because the pizza owner violated the burger owner's right to a fair competition.
option_j: Yes, because the pizza owner interfered with his right of peaceful enjoyment and disrupted his business activities.

question_text: A student at a private university sued the university in federal court for negligence after he fell from scaffolding in a university-owned theater building. At trial, after briefing from both parties, the court permitted the jury to hear testimony that there had been several previous accidents in the same building. The jury found for the student, and the university appealed. One of the university's arguments on appeal is that the testimony about the previous accidents should have been excluded as irrelevant and highly prejudicial. Which standard of review applies to this argument?

A commissioner of a sports league was charged with embezzling funds by increasing the amount of his allocated travel expenses without the approval of a majority of the team owners. In accordance with the bylaws of the league's charter, the commissioner was required to obtain the approval of at least half the owners before raising his expense allocation. At trial, the commissioner seeks to testify that his predecessor told him that he had authority under the league's charter to raise his expense allocation. The commissioner's testimony is
option_a: admissible, to show the precedent set by the predecessor.
option_b: inadmissible, because it is second-hand information.
option_c: inadmissible as hearsay not within any recognized exception.
option_d: inadmissible, because the league's charter is the best evidence.
option_e: admissible, as it can prove the commissioner's innocence.
option_f: inadmissible, because the predecessor's statement cannot be verified.
option_g: admissible, to show that the commissioner lacked criminal intent.
option_h: inadmissible, as it is not relevant to the case.
option_i: admissible as evidence of the routine practice of an organization.
option_j: admissible, as it gives insight into the league's charter interpretation.

question_text: On Thanksgiving Day, a father was carving a turkey for his family when he seriously cut his hand. The knife severed an artery, causing the father to lose a lot of blood. The mother telephoned their family doctor, who instructed her to drive the father to the local hospital. The doctor indicated that he would meet them there. A short time later at the hospital, the doctor treated the father and bandaged his hand. However, due to the fact that the father had lost a considerable amount of blood, the doctor ordered an immediate blood transfusion. After determining that the father's blood type was A-positive, the doctor orally instructed a nurse, who was employed by the hospital, to obtain the necessary blood for the transfusion. The nurse followed the doctor's instructions and telephoned the hospital's blood bank department and ordered a unit of A-positive blood. The person in the blood bank who received the nurse's call correctly wrote down the order for A-positive blood. However, someone working at the blood bank, whose identity cannot be determined, made an error in filling the order and labeling the unit of blood for the father. As a result, the blood bank sent a unit of B-positive blood mislabeled as A-positive blood. After being administered the wrong blood, the father died. The hospital has a written rule in effect whereby all orders for blood transfusions must be made in writing by the physician in charge of the patient's treatment. Although the doctor was aware of the hospital rule, he, like many other doctors at the hospital, frequently ordered blood transfusions by oral instructions. If the mother asserts a wrongful death action against the doctor, she will most likely
option_a: prevail, because the doctor would be responsible for the blood bank's error in administering the wrong blood.
option_b: not prevail, because the unidentified person in the blood bank who was responsible for sending the wrong blood type was not an employee of the doctor.
option_c: not prevail, because the hospital, not the doctor, is responsible for the errors of its employees.
option_d: prevail, because the doctor had a duty of care to ensure the correct blood type was administered.
option_e: prevail, because the doctor's failure to follow the hospital's rules indirectly led to the father's death.
option_f: not prevail, because the doctor did not personally administer the wrong blood type.
option_g: not prevail, because the doctor acted in the same fashion as many other doctors at the hospital.
option_h: not prevail, because the doctor gave correct instructions to the nurse.
option_i: prevail, because the doctor did not follow the hospital's rule regarding blood transfusion orders.
option_j: prevail, because the doctor should have ensured that the nurse followed the hospital's rule for blood transfusion orders.

question_text: A tenant was cleaning out the bedroom of his apartment as his lease had expired. He carried out his waterbed, dresser, and all of his clothes. As the tenant inspected the room one last time, he noticed a bookcase that he had screwed into the wall when he first moved in. While he pondered whether to remove the bookcase, the tenant concluded that it constituted a fixture and, therefore, was a permanent part of the realty. Nonetheless, the tenant decided that the bookcase would fit nicely in his new apartment, so he removed it from the bedroom wall. When the landlord learned that the tenant had removed the bookcase, he contacted the tenant and demanded that he return it to the apartment. The tenant refused. If the tenant is subsequently prosecuted for larceny, he will most likely be found
option_a: guilty, because removal of the bookcase caused damage to the property.
option_b: not guilty, because the bookcase was not permanently affixed to the property.
option_c: guilty, because the tenant did not have the right to remove fixtures from the property.
option_d: not guilty, because the bookcase was a fixture.
option_e: guilty, because the chattel was the personal property of the landlord.
option_f: not guilty, because the tenant cannot be found guilty of theft for removing his own property.
option_g: guilty, because severance of the bookcase from the wall was sufficient asportation to constitute larceny.
option_h: guilty, because the tenant did not obtain consent from the landlord to remove the bookcase.
option_i: not guilty, because the landlord did not explicitly state that the bookcase could not be removed.

question_text: A wife was divorced from her husband. The wife and the husband had a daughter who was 12 years of age. The daughter lived with the wife, but the husband frequently visited his daughter at her house. One morning, the husband came to his ex-wife's home to see his daughter. He and his daughter were outside on the patio chatting when the wife noticed her ex-husband's briefcase lying on the kitchen floor. The husband had entered the house carrying his briefcase, which he placed down before going out to see his daughter on the patio. Seeing the briefcase, the wife decided to open it. Inside she found a large envelope. She decided to keep it, thinking it contained money. Later that night after her ex-husband left, she opened the envelope and found a large amount of cocaine. The wife called the police who came and confiscated the drugs. The husband was subsequently charged with possession of cocaine. The husband has filed a motion to exclude the cocaine from being introduced into evidence. His motion should be
option_a: granted, because the police failed to secure a search warrant before the seizure of the cocaine.
option_b: denied, because the wife had the right to open the briefcase left in her house.
option_c: denied, because the husband left the briefcase in the kitchen in plain view.
option_d: denied, because the police were called after the cocaine was found.
option_e: denied, because the husband had no reasonable expectation of privacy at his ex-wife's home.
option_f: denied, because the initial seizure was made by a private individual.
option_g: granted, because the cocaine was found in his personal briefcase.
option_h: granted, because the husband did not give consent for the briefcase to be opened.
option_i: granted, because the wife had no right to open the briefcase without the husband's permission.
option_j: granted, because the seizure violated the husband's right of privacy.

question_text: Knowing that a homeowner was away on vacation, two men decided to burglarize her home. Since they didn't have a car, the men asked the defendant to drive them to the home. The two men did not tell the defendant what they intended to do there. The defendant drove them to the house. While the defendant waited in the car, the two men entered the home by using a master key to unlock the front door. They then stole several items of expensive jewelry from the bedroom. Concealing the jewelry in their pockets, they left the house and got back into the defendant's car. Unaware of the theft, the defendant drove the two men back to their apartments. The next day, the two men pawned the jewelry for $5,000. Two weeks later, the two men gave the defendant $500 and told him for the first time about the burglary. The defendant kept the money and did not report the theft to the police. If the defendant is subsequently prosecuted, he should be found
option_a: guilty of aiding and abetting a burglary but not guilty of receiving stolen property.
option_b: not guilty of receiving stolen property but guilty of aiding and abetting a burglary.
option_c: guilty of receiving stolen property but not guilty of aiding and abetting a burglary.
option_d: guilty of burglary, receiving stolen property, and aiding and abetting a burglary.
option_e: guilty of aiding and abetting a burglary and of receiving stolen property.
option_f: guilty of burglary and of receiving stolen property.
option_g: guilty of burglary but not guilty of receiving stolen property.
option_h: not guilty of either burglary or receiving stolen property.
option_i: not guilty of burglary but guilty of aiding and abetting a burglary.

question_text: An officer stopped a car for having a burned out headlight and license plate light. When the driver could not produce a driver's license, the officer asked him if he minded if they searched the vehicle. The officer did not advise the driver that he had a right to refuse consent or that if he refused his decision would be honored, but there was nothing duplicitous or coercive in the officer's conduct. The driver smiled and said "of course, no problem." The officer found stolen bank checks under the rear seat. In a later suppression motion, the driver claimed that his consent was not voluntary. What will the court most likely decide on that issue?
option_a: The consent was voluntarily given and was not the result of coercion or duress.
option_b: The consent was involuntary because the officer did not have a warrant to search the car.
option_c: The consent was involuntary because it was the product of implied duress.
option_d: The consent was involuntary because the driver did not explicitly say the words "I consent."
option_e: The consent was involuntary because the officer found stolen bank checks in the car.
option_f: The consent was involuntary because the officer did not advise the driver of his right to refuse.
option_g: The consent became voluntary as a matter of law when the driver failed to produce a driver's license.
option_h: The consent was voluntary because the officer asked politely.
option_i: The consent was voluntarily given because the driver smiled and was cooperative.
option_j: The consent was involuntary as the driver was not informed about the consequences of refusal.

question_text: One evening, a husband received a telephone call from an unidentified source who informed him that his wife was having an affair with his friend. As a result, the husband drove to his friend's house with a loaded shotgun. Believing that his friend was in an upstairs bedroom, the husband fired the shotgun through the dining room window, as he only wanted to teach him a lesson. Unbeknownst to the husband, his friend's wife was in the dining room and suffered a minor gunshot wound. The husband should be found
option_a: guilty of assault on his friend's wife only.
option_b: guilty of attempted murder of both his friend and his friend's wife.
option_c: guilty of reckless endangerment of both his friend and his friend's wife.
option_d: guilty of attempted murder of his friend's wife only.
option_e: guilty of attempted murder of his friend only.
option_f: guilty of attempted murder of his friend and assault on his friend's wife.
option_g: guilty of attempted manslaughter of both his friend and his friend's wife.
option_h: guilty of attempted manslaughter of his friend only.
option_i: not guilty of attempted murder of either his friend or his friend's wife.
option_j: not guilty due to lack of intent to kill.

question_text: A state in which several popular ski resorts are located has enacted a law requiring state certification of ski instructors. Under the law, applicants for certification must attend a monthlong course, pass a test, and pay a fee of $1,000. The stated purpose of the law is to "promote uniformity in the methods of ski instruction at the state's ski areas." Shortly before the law's enactment, when the state did not require certification of ski instructors, a woman moved to the state intending to find employment as a ski instructor. The woman had been a ski instructor in another state for many years. But since her move, even though ski resorts in the state have found her to be highly qualified, no resort will offer her a contract for employment as a ski instructor because she has not received the required state certification. As applied to the woman, does the state certification requirement constitute a violation of the contracts clause?
option_a: Yes, because the requirement substantially impairs her ability to contract for employment in the absence of an important government objective.
option_b: Yes, because the law was enacted after she moved to the state.
option_c: No, because the law is necessary for the safety of skiers.
option_d: No, because her ability to contract for employment with the state is not being impaired.
option_e: Yes, because the law restricts her freedom to engage in her profession.
option_f: Yes, because, as applied to an otherwise qualified ski instructor, the requirement is not rationally related to a legitimate government interest.
option_g: Yes, because the certification requirement is too costly and time-consuming.
option_h: No, because the certification requirement is intended to standardize ski instruction.
option_i: No, because she has no existing contract that is being impaired.
option_j: No, because the law applies to all ski instructors equally.

question_text: A vehicle operator was driving on a highway at night. His girlfriend was in the passenger seat. They were returning from a college night class. She was smoking marijuana, blowing it out the open window, and not sharing it with the driver. The driver fell asleep, causing the car to veer off the highway and go into a ditch where it landed upside-down. Both the driver and passenger were seriously injured. She sued the operator for negligence. The operator filed a counterclaim asking for damages against the passenger by arguing that the passenger was herself negligent by "smoking weed" and causing him to fall asleep. Is the court likely to grant the passenger's motion to dismiss the counterclaim?
option_a: No, because the passenger was breaking the law, making her negligent per se.
option_b: Yes, because the passenger was not actively participating in a joint enterprise and did not do enough to contribute to the cause of the accident.
option_c: No, because the driver has the right to file a counterclaim and it should be considered by the court.
option_d: No, because the passenger was aware of the driver's state and should have taken steps to prevent the accident.
option_e: No, because the passenger was acting irresponsibly and put both herself and the driver at risk.
option_f: Yes, because the law does not recognize the effects of secondhand marijuana smoke as a valid cause of accidents.
option_g: No, because the driver has properly alleged a joint enterprise situation where the passenger is liable for her share of the negligence in causing the accident.

question_text: A company contracted with a builder to construct a new corporate headquarters for a fixed price of $100 million. At the time of the contract, structural steel was widely available and was included in the contract as a $6 million item. Before work began on the project, tornado damage shut down the production facility of the biggest structural steel supplier in the country, and the price of structural steel increased by 20% as a result. The builder informed the company of the steel price increase, and the parties then orally agreed to increase the project price to $101 million. The builder proceeded with construction and delivered the project on time. The company paid the builder $100 million but refused to pay the additional $1 million. If the builder sues the company for $1 million, is the builder likely to prevail?
option_a: No, because the price change of structural steel was not significant enough to warrant a contract modification.
option_b: No, because there was no consideration for the modification of the contract.
option_c: No, because the modification was never reduced to a writing signed by the party to be charged.
option_d: No, because the company did not sign a new contract reflecting the price change.
option_e: Yes, because the modification was fair and equitable in view of the unanticipated increase in the price of structural steel.
option_f: Yes, because the builder completed the project on time despite the unforeseen circumstances.
option_g: Yes, because the builder had no control over the price increase of structural steel.
option_h: Yes, because the companyÕs promise was supported by consideration.
option_i: Yes, because the company was aware of the price increase and verbally agreed to it.
option_j: No, because the builder should have anticipated potential price fluctuations in materials.

question_text: A high school student was running for president of the student council. He was a top scholar who had been selected to be the class valedictorian. Inexplicably, he gave a campaign speech at a school assembly that was filled with graphic stories and anecdotes about the imagined sexual adventures and prowess of certain teachers and students. Several teachers and students complained. The principal suspended him for five days and retracted the valedictorian honors. The student, with his parents, sued in federal court, seeking an injunction and damages based on denial of First Amendment free speech rights. The federal courts ruled for the student but the case came before the U.S. Supreme Court for final decision. Did the Supreme Court uphold the relief granted to the student?
option_a: No, the school exercised its appropriate function of trying to restrict the use of vulgarity and offensive discourse in a high school setting where free speech may be limited.
option_b: Yes, in today's society sexual images and sexual humor is so intertwined into the fabric of society that it is something that has to be tolerated in the interest of free speech.
option_c: Yes, the Supreme Court upheld the relief granted to the student because it was a personal expression and did not incite violence or hate.
option_d: Yes, the student's rights to free speech should be upheld regardless of the content of his speech.
option_e: Yes, despite some inconvenience to teachers and students, the immature presentation must be tolerated in deference to the need to provide platforms for free speech to students.
option_f: Yes, the school should not have the power to restrict any form of speech as it impedes on the student's rights to express themselves.
option_g: No, the student had no rights to free speech, especially considering the violent disruptions in the schools in recent years.

question_text: A man was in jail after being arrested for burglary. When the police attempted to question him, the man invoked his Miranda rights and refused to answer any questions. The man was subsequently tried, convicted, and sentenced to a prison term for the burglary. Three years later, while the man was serving his prison sentence for the burglary, a police detective from a nearby town questioned him about an unsolved homicide. The detective did not know that the man had invoked Miranda at an earlier time. that the man had invoked Miranda at an earlier time. The man waived his Miranda rights and made several incriminating statements to the detective. When he was later charged with the homicide, the man moved to suppress these statements, claiming that his earlier refusal to waive his Miranda rights should have been honored. Should the court suppress the statements?
option_a: No, because the man's prior invocation of his Miranda rights did not preclude the later interrogation.
option_b: Yes, because the man was under duress while being interrogated in prison.
option_c: Yes, because the man had earlier invoked his Miranda rights, and the police were not permitted to resume questioning, even after a time lapse of years.
option_d: Yes, because the man was not informed of his Miranda rights before the second interrogation.
option_e: No, because the man was serving a prison sentence, which nullifies his Miranda rights.
option_f: Yes, because the detective should have been informed of the man's earlier invocation of his Miranda rights.
option_g: No, because the detective was unaware that the man had originally invoked his Miranda rights.
option_h: Yes, because the man was incarcerated, and his earlier invocation of his Miranda rights shielded him from further questioning until shielded him from further questioning until he was released.
option_i: No, because the new charge of homicide is unrelated to the original burglary charge.
option_j: No, because the man willingly waived his Miranda rights during the second interrogation.

question_text: A defendant went to a convenience store intending to rob it. The defendant had a gun inside his coat pocket. When the defendant entered the store, the owner saw that he had his hand in his coat pocket. Although the owner did not actually see the gun, he noticed a bulge in the defendant's coat pocket. Paranoid because of a rash of recent robberies, the owner said, "Please don't hurt me 11 do anything you want. " The owner then fainted and fell to the floor. The defendant walked behind the counter and opened the cash register. He took the money from the register and left the store. If the defendant is charged with robbery, he should be found
option_a: guilty, because the defendant entered the store with a gun in his possession.
option_b: guilty, because the owner was placed in fear.
option_c: not guilty, because the owner gave permission to take the money.
option_d: guilty, because the defendant had a weapon.
option_e: not guilty, because the defendant did not take any money from the victim's person.
option_f: not guilty, because the owner did not see the gun.
option_g: guilty, because the defendant took money from the store.
option_h: not guilty, because the owner did not directly interact with the defendant.
option_i: guilty, because the defendant intended to rob the store.
option_j: not guilty, because the defendant did not make any threat of force.

question_text: Two days before his home was to be sold at a foreclosure sale, a homeowner obtained a temporary restraining order (TRO) in federal court that prevented his lender from proceeding with the sale for 14 days or until a preliminary injunction hearing could take place, whichever was sooner. When a preliminary injunction hearing could not be scheduled within the original 14-day period, the court extended the TRO for another 30 days. The lender appealed the court's order extending the TRO. The homeowner has moved to dismiss the appeal. Is the appellate court likely to dismiss the appeal?
option_a: No, because the TRO is considered a final order and therefore appealable.
option_b: No, because the lender has the right to appeal any order that prevents the foreclosure sale.
option_c: Yes, because the preliminary injunction hearing could not be scheduled within the original 14-day period.
option_d: No, because a TRO is immediately appealable.
option_e: Yes, because the lender did not have the right to appeal the TRO extension.
option_f: No, because the 30-day extension makes the TRO equivalent to a preliminary injunction and therefore appealable.
option_g: Yes, because the court did not have jurisdiction to extend the TRO.
option_h: No, because the homeowner's motion to dismiss is not valid.
option_i: Yes, because there is no final judgment from which an appeal may be taken.

question_text: After a severe thunderstorm had struck the area, a homeowner discovered that her roof had suffered extensive damage. She telephoned a roofer to inquire about having the necessary repairs done to her roof. The roofer agreed to finish the work within 30 days, and the homeowner promised to pay$10,000. Which of the following statements is most accurate?
option_a: The contract would be unenforceable, because it was not written and signed by both parties.
option_b: The performance of the roofer would be a constructive condition precedent to the performance by the homeowner.
option_c: The performance by the homeowner would be an implied condition subsequent to the performance by the roofer.
option_d: The performance by the homeowner would be a constructive condition precedent to the performance by the roofer.
option_e: The performances of the homeowner and the roofer would be constructive concurrent conditions.
option_f: The contract would be unenforceable, because it was oral.
option_g: The performance of the roofer would be an implied condition subsequent to the performance by the homeowner.
option_h: The performances of the homeowner and the roofer would be independent conditions.
option_i: The performances of the homeowner and the roofer would be express concurrent conditions.

question_text: What is the meaning of cultural relativism?
option_a: Cultural relativism posits that the application of human rights is relative to the laws in each nation
option_b: Cultural relativism posits that laws in each nation should validate the existence and practice of human rights
option_c: Cultural relativism posits that human rights are determined by global consensus
option_d: Culture relativism posits that human rights have a cultural origin
option_e: Cultural relativism posits that all cultures are objectively equal in value
option_f: Cultural relativism posits that human rights should be universally the same in every culture
option_g: Cultural relativism posits that human rights are determined by religious beliefs
option_h: Culture relativism posits that human rights apply equally to all without restrictions
option_i: Cultural relativism posits that the validity of human rights depends on individual beliefs
option_j: Cultural relativism posits that local culture should validate the existence and practice of all human rights

question_text: Rainmaking Lawfirm regularly purchased its office supplies from catalogs. Marty Manager saw an advertising catalog from Costco offering 10,000 envelopes for $1,000 CIF. He immediately sent a purchase order which stated "our law firm accepts your $1,000 offer for 10,000 envelopes for $1,000 CIF." Costco then sent Rainmaking an order confirmation which stated "Envelope order acceptance conditional upon a loading charge of $50 per thousand envelopes." If the parties disagree on the proper contract relationship, a court would likely rule:
option_a: A contract at $1,000 because the offer terms CIF means cost, insurance and freight including all loading charges.
option_b: A contract at $1,500 because the loading charges are to be included.
option_c: No contract because the order confirmation was a counteroffer which was not accepted.
option_d: No contract because the purchase order was the offer and, under the mirror image rule, cannot be deviated from.

question_text: A widow owned an apartment building. Several years ago, a student, in a signed writing, entered a three-year lease agreement with the widow. The student agreed to pay $800 monthly rent for the third-floor apartment in the widow's building. The original three-year term had long since expired, but the student had continued to pay his rent, and the widow continued to accept it. If the widow had chosen to evict the student immediately upon the end of the three-year period, and before she had accepted the next rent check, the student's strongest argument to prevent eviction, if true, would have been that
option_a: the student had a right to stay as he had been residing there for more than three years.
option_b: the widow had not given the student requisite notice for terminating a term of years.
option_c: the student had made improvements to the property, thus extending the leasehold.
option_d: the student had a verbal agreement with the widow to extend the lease.
option_e: the terms of the leasehold contract were binding on both parties.
option_f: the widow had accepted rent for the following month before the three-year period ended.
option_g: the student was protected from eviction due to his status as a student.
option_h: the widow had not clearly communicated the end of the lease term.
option_i: the widow had not given the student requisite notice for terminating a periodic tenancy.
option_j: the leasehold contract was void ab initio.

question_text: A landlord is the owner in fee simple of a tract of land on which is situated a large office building. The landlord leases the land and building thereon to a tenant for a term of seven years, commencing on August 1, 2001, and terminating at midnight on July 31, 2008. The lease contains the following provisions"(1) The tenant covenants to keep the building on the leased premises insured against fire in the amount of $100,000. "After three years of the lease had expired, the tenant assigned the entire balance of the lease period to a doctor, who took immediate possession of the leased property. Then in 2005, the doctor assigned his leasehold interest to a dentist. The dentist went into possession, but failed to pay any rent for two months. Soon afterwards, a fire spread from an adjoining building to the leased property, completely destroying the building thereon. While the tenant was in possession of the leased property, he carried a fire insurance policy on the premises in the amount of $100,000. However, the tenant allowed the policy to lapse after his assignment to the doctor. The doctor did carry insurance on the leased building, but only in the amount of $50,000. When the dentist took possession of the building, he did not obtain any fire insurance. The landlord learned that the building was not insured at the time of the fire as per the original agreement. In an action by the landlord against the tenant to recover for the fire loss, the landlord will most probably
option_a: recover, because the fire spread from an adjoining building, making the owner of that building responsible for the damage.
option_b: not recover, because the covenant to provide fire insurance did not run with the land.
option_c: recover, because in accordance with the Rule in Spencer's Case, the covenant to maintain fire insurance would "touch and concern" the land.
option_d: not recover, because the dentist, as assignee, would be liable for the fire loss.
option_e: not recover, because the doctor had partially fulfilled the obligation by providing insurance coverage of $50,000.
option_f: not recover, because the tenant had fulfilled his obligation by maintaining insurance during his possession of the property.
option_g: recover, because the dentist took possession of the property without obtaining any fire insurance.
option_h: recover, because the tenant's obligation to maintain fire insurance did not terminate after his assignment to the doctor.
option_i: recover, because the dentist failed to pay rent, which technically broke the lease agreement.
option_j: not recover, because the tenant no longer had control over the property after his assignment to the doctor.

question_text: The President announced that a newly created military rapid deployment force would be engaging in joint training exercises with the military forces in a foreign country. Following the President's announcement, Congress enacted a statute that unequivocally prohibited "the U. S. Armed Forces from conducting military exercises with foreign military forces unless Congress has received notice of such intention to take such action at least three months before the joint military exercises become effective. "This statute is most likely
option_a: constitutional, because the President does not have the authority to engage in foreign military exercises without Congressional approval.
option_b: unconstitutional, due to the violation of separation of powers.
option_c: constitutional, because it falls under Congress's ability to declare war.
option_d: constitutional, because the President, in this instance, has not been called by Congress into actual service as Commander-in-Chief.
option_e: constitutional, because of Congress's power to regulate the Armed Forces.
option_f: unconstitutional, because of the President's authority to execute the laws of the United States.
option_g: unconstitutional, because the President has the exclusive power to determine military strategy.
option_h: unconstitutional, because of the President's authority as Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces.
option_i: constitutional, because of Congressional power under the War Powers Act.
option_j: unconstitutional, because it infringes on the President's power to maintain national security.

question_text: After being passed over for a promotion, an aeronautic engineer became a whistleblower. He contacted a government agency to state that the company for which he worked was submitting false safety reports on newly built aircraft. When the company learned that the engineer was leaking this information to the government agency, he was fired from his job. Afterward, the engineer sued the company for wrongful termination of employment. During the discovery stage of litigation, the engineer was deposed by the company's attorney. In his deposition, the engineer stated that the company submitted false safety reports to the government agency to cover up structural defects in its aircraft. A pilot was injured when one of the company'sairplanes he was piloting crashed. The pilot asserteda strict products liability tort against the company. At thal, the pilot seeks to introduce into evidence portions of the engineer's deposition from his wrongful termination lawsuit against the company. Assume that the engineer is unavailable to testify at trial. Upon objection by the company, the trial court judge should rule the engineer's deposition testimony
option_b: inadmissible as hearsay within the business records exception.
option_c: inadmissible due to the engineer's unavailability.
option_d: admissible as a statement against interest.
option_f: inadmissible, because the company did not have the opportunity to cross-examine the engineer on the liability issue for which the statement is now being offered.
option_h: inadmissible because the engineer's deposition is biased.
option_i: inadmissible as hearsay not within any recognized exception.
option_j: admissible as a statement of personal or family history.

question_text: A defendant has been charged with making a false statement to a federally insured financial institution to secure a loan. At trial, the prosecutor calls the defendant's wife as a willing witness to testify that the defendant told her in confidence that he had misrepresented his assets on the loan application. The defendant objects to his wife's testimony. Should the testimony be admitted?
option_a: No, because even though the wife is a willing witness, the defendant has the right to exclude confidential marital communications in federal court.
option_b: Yes, because the defendant's right to a fair trial outweighs any confidential marital communication.
option_c: Yes, because while the adverse testimonial privilege is recognized in federal court, the marital communications privilege is not.
option_d: Yes, because in federal court the right not to testify belongs to the testifying spouse, and she is a willing witness.
option_e: No, because the wife's testimony is hearsay and not admissible in court.
option_f: Yes, because the defendant's fraudulent actions supersede any marital communication privileges.
option_g: No, but only if the law of the state where the defendant and his wife reside recognizes a privilege for confidential marital communications.
option_h: No, because the defendant's wife is not a qualified expert on loan applications.
option_i: No, because the nature of the crime excludes the admittance of testimonies from family members.
option_j: Yes, because the prosecution has the right to call any willing witness to testify.

question_text: A landlord owned a large building in the city. On January iS, the landlord leased the building to atenant for a period of 20 years at a rental of $10,000 per month. The leasehold agreement between the landlord and tenant provided that the latter was not permitted "to assign this lease to anyone except a corporation with an A' credit rating from a well- known credit rating company. " On February 1 of the following year, the tenant leased the premises to a corporation that did not have the required credit rating. The tenant corporation lease was for a period of five years, with a rental of $15,000 per month, payable by the corporation to the tenant. In addition, the corporation agreed to abide "by all of the terms and conditions of the lease between the landlord and the tenant. "One year later, the corporation leased the premises to a health club for the balance of the term of the corporation tenant lease. The health club owner took possession of the said premises on February 1, the same day that the corporation vacated its occupancy. Pursuant to the corporation health club leasehold agreement, the latter was obligated to pay a monthly rental of $17,500 directly to the corporation. The health club has a B' credit rating with the well-known credit rating company. For one year, the health club paid $17,500, each month directly to the corporation. During that same period, the corporation continued to pay $15,000 each month to the tenant, while the latter paid $10,000 (each month) to the landlord. The landlord knew about the leases to the corporation and the health club and protested promptly, but took no further action, apparently satisfied as long as he received his $10,000 per month from the tenant. Thereafter, the health club abandoned the premises and stopped paying rent to the corporation. After the health club discontinued paying rent, the corporation stopped paying rent to the tenant. When the tenant failed to receive his rent, he, too, stopped paying rent to the landlord. If the landlord brings suit to recover for past rent due, which of the following is most accurate?
option_a: The landlord may only recover against the health club for past rent due.
option_b: The landlord may recover against the health club for past rent due.
option_c: The landlord may recover against the tenant and the health club, jointly and severally, for past rent due.
option_d: The landlord may recover against the health club and the corporation, but not the tenant, for past rent due.
option_e: The landlord may only recover against the corporation for past rent due.
option_f: The landlord may recover against the tenant and the corporation, jointly and severally, for past rent due.
option_g: The landlord may recover against the corporation for past rent due.
option_h: The landlord may recover against the tenant for past rent due.
option_i: The landlord cannot recover against anyone for past rent due.
option_j: The landlord may recover against the corporation and the health club, jointly and severally,for past rent due.

question_text: A 26-year-old graduate student knew that a classmate was a staunch supporter of animal rights. As they were leaving class one afternoon, the graduate student walked beside the classmate and chanted, "Animal experimentation is great. " The classmate angrily confronted the graduate student and said, "Let's settle this outside in a fist fight. " The graduate student replied, "You're on. "The two students went outside and "squared off. " The graduate student threw the first punch and hit the classmate in the face with his bare fist. Unknown to the graduate student, the classmate placed a set of brass knuckles on his fist and hit the graduate student in the face with them. The force of the blow broke the graduate student's nose. It was later determined that the use of the brass knuckles inflicted exactly the same damage as if the classmate had hit the graduate student with his bare fist. The best argument for rejecting the defense of consent in an action by the graduate student for either assault or battery is that
option_a: the classmate was angered by the graduate student's chants, hence reacted excessively.
option_b: the classmate's punch would have inflicted the same harm with or without the use of the brass knuckles.
option_c: the graduate student was unaware of the classmate's use of brass knuckles.
option_d: the graduate student consented to a fist fight, not a fight involving weapons.
option_e: the damage inflicted by the classmate was more severe than what the graduate student had consented to.
option_f: the classmate's use of brass knuckles was a premeditated act of violence.
option_g: the graduate student's insults provoked the classmate's use of excessive force.
option_h: the use of brass knuckles is generally illegal, regardless of consent.
option_i: the classmate was only acting in self-defense.
option_j: the classmate's use of the brass knuckles exceeded consent under the circumstances.

question_text: A landlord leased a commercial building to a tenant for five years. Rent was payable on the first day of each month, and the landlord retained the right to terminate the lease if the tenant defaulted. The lease term ended 18 months ago. However, the tenant has stayed in possession of the building and has continued to pay the rent on time. The landlord has continued to accept the rent. The fair rental value of the building is now substantially more than what the tenant has been paying, and the landlord recently found a third party who will pay the higher rent. When the tenant paid the rent six weeks ago, the landlord accepted paid the rent six weeks ago, the landlord accepted the rent but told the tenant in writing that she had to vacate at the end of the month. The tenant, who is still in possession of the building, has informed the landlord that she does not want to move and is willing to pay the current fair rental value. The landlord has asked his lawyer for advice regarding the right to possession. Who should the lawyer say is entitled to possession?
option_a: The tenant, because she has not violated any terms of the original lease.
option_b: The tenant, because she has stated that she is willing to pay the current fair rental value.
option_c: The tenant, because she has been making regular payments and is still in possession of the building.
option_d: The tenant, because she has the right to remain in the building as long as she continues to pay rent.
option_e: The landlord, because he gave proper notice to terminate the tenancy.
option_f: The landlord, because the lease term ended 18 months ago.
option_g: The landlord, because he has found a third party willing to pay a higher rent.
option_h: The tenant, because the landlord has not shown good cause to terminate the tenancy.
option_i: The landlord, because the tenant has overstayed the lease term.
option_j: The landlord, because he retained the right to reenter.

question_text: A man was arrested and charged with sexually assaulting a child. At trial, in its case-in-chief, the prosecution seeks to introduce evidence that the man had twice been previously involved in similar cases with other children. The prosecution argues that these past crimes demonstrate the likelihood that the man molested this child. Defense counsel objects to the evidence regarding other children. The judge should find the evidence
option_a: inadmissible, because the prosecution cannot initiate evidence of bad character.
option_b: admissible, because the evidence of past crimes is relevant to the case at hand.
option_c: inadmissible, because the evidence of past crimes is prejudicial and would bias the jury.
option_d: admissible, because the man's past crimes demonstrate a pattern of behavior.
option_e: inadmissible, because past crimes may not be introduced to prove disposition to commit certain acts.
option_f: inadmissible, because the man's past crimes are not similar enough to the current charge.
option_g: admissible, because a common plan or scheme may be proven by other similar criminal acts.
option_h: admissible, as long as the evidence of past crimes does not outweigh its probative value.
option_i: inadmissible, because past crimes are not relevant to the case at hand.
option_j: admissible, because the man's past crimes may be introduced to show that he is more likely to have committed the crime with which he is charged.

question_text: The operator of a hot dog stand sent the following purchase order to a bakery: "We are expecting an increase in business over the upcoming holidays. Please ship immediately 36 dozen 10-inch hot dog buns at your current list price of $6 per dozen. "The day after receiving the purchase order, the bakery shipped 24 dozen 10-inch hot dog buns and 12 dozen 8-inch hot dog buns. The owner of the bakery had instructed his staff to include a message that the bakery did not have enough 10-inch hot dog buns to fill the order, so they were including the 8-inch hot dog buns as an accommodation. However, the shipping department forgot to include the message with the shipment. Which of the following is the most accurate statement regarding the operator's legal rights following receipt of the hot dog buns?
option_a: The operator may either accept or reject all of the hot dog buns upon seasonable notice to the bakery, but it cannot accept only the 10-inch hot dog buns without the bakery's approval.
option_b: The operator may either accept or reject all of the hot dog buns, or, provided the bakery gives seasonable notice that the shipment was made for accommodation only, the operator may accept any combination of hot dog buns and reject the rest.
option_c: The operator may either accept or reject all of the hot dog buns, or accept the 10-inch buns and reject the 8-inch buns, but only with written approval from the bakery.
option_d: The operator may accept the 10-inch buns and reject the 8-inch buns, without any obligation to notify the bakery.
option_e: The operator may accept any combination of hot dog buns without the need to notify the bakery.
option_f: The operator can only reject the shipment if the bakery provides notice that the shipment was made for accommodation only.
option_g: The operator may either accept or reject all of the hot dog buns, or accept the 10-inch buns and reject the 8-inch buns, but it cannot accept any combination of the hot dog buns.
option_h: The operator can reject the 8-inch buns and demand a refund, without providing any notice to the bakery.
option_i: The operator may either accept or reject all of the hot dog buns, or accept any commercial unit and reject the rest, but must give the bakery seasonable notice of either total or partial rejection.
option_j: The operator must accept all of the hot dog buns, and cannot reject any part of the shipment.

question_text: A homeowner contracted in writing with a kitchen contractor to renovate her kitchen for $25,000, "subject to the homeowner's complete personal satisfaction." The contractor replaced the cabinets, flooring, and countertops and then sought payment from the homeowner. The homeowner paid the contractor only $20,000, truthfully saying that she did not like the finish on the cabinets and was therefore not satisfied. If the contractor sues the homeowner for the balance of the contract price, will the contractor be likely to prevail?
option_a: Yes, because the homeowner cannot base the payment on personal satisfaction.
option_b: Yes, because the homeowner breached the covenant of good faith and fair dealing by rejecting the cabinets without justification.
option_c: No, because the contractor breached his duty of good faith and fair dealing by supplying unsatisfactory materials.
option_d: No, because the contractor should have ensured the homeowner's satisfaction before seeking payment.
option_e: Yes, because the contractor has the right to sue for the balance of the contract price.
option_f: Yes, because the homeowner was the first party to breach the contract.
option_g: No, because a condition to the homeowner's obligation to pay was not satisfied.
option_h: No, because the homeowner was not satisfied with the finish on the cabinets.
option_i: Yes, because the contractor fulfilled his part of the contract by completing the renovation.
option_j: No, because the homeowner has a right to withhold payment if not completely satisfied.

question_text: A landlord owns a three-story building. The landlord leased the building and the surrounding property to a tenant for a term of six years, commencing on December 1, 2003 and terminating on November 30, 2009. On April 15, 2004, a neighbor, who owned an adjoining parcel of land, was burning leaves and underbrush on his land. There was a high wind blowing in the direction of the land that the landlord leased to the tenant, and the wind carried the burning leaves onto the tenant's property. The building caught fire and caused $150,000 in fire damages. Because of the fire, the tenant has not occupied the leased premises since the date of the fire because the building was rendered uninhabitable. This state is a common law jurisdiction. In addition, the controlling Statute of Limitations for initiating an action for damages due to negligence is five years from the time the cause of damage occurs. On May 1, 2004, the landlord brings suit against the tenant asserting breach of contract for failing to pay the rent. Judgment should be for
option_a: the tenant, because the landlord failed to provide a habitable living space.
option_b: the landlord, because the tenant is responsible for all damages to the property, regardless of the cause.
option_c: the tenant, because the fire was caused by a third party, not by the tenant's negligence.
option_d: the tenant, because it would be inequitable to enforce the rental provision in the lease because the premises was rendered uninhabitable.
option_e: the tenant, because the fire damage made it impossible for the tenant to use the property for its intended purpose.
option_f: the landlord, because the lease contract does not contain a clause relieving the tenant from paying rent in case of damages caused by external factors.
option_g: the tenant, because an increasing number of states have enacted statutes relieving a tenant of his obligation to pay rent where the premises are rendered uninhabitable by fire or other acts of God.
option_h: the landlord, because a tenant remains liable to pay rent, even though as a result of the fire, the property was rendered uninhabitable.
option_i: the landlord, because the covenant to pay rent runs with the land.
option_j: the landlord, because the tenant is responsible for the fire as it happened on their leased property.

question_text: An on-duty Police Officer hears breaking glass and a blaring alarm, and sees Stan run out of a darkened liquor store at 3 a.m. Stan runs around the corner, out of Officer's view, and hops into a car. Officer sees Stan drive away and pursues, lights flashing. Stan pulls over and Officer arrests him. Officer sees nothing in the passenger compartment, but pops the trunk and finds cash and a baseball bat. Stan's motion to suppress the cash and baseball bat as evidence will likely be
option_a: Denied, because the Officer had a reasonable suspicion that Stan was involved in a crime.
option_b: Granted, because the arrest was illegal and therefore the evidence is considered to be fruit of the poisonous tree.
option_c: Granted, because the Officer did not have a search warrant to check the trunk of the car.
option_d: Granted, because the Officer did not have a valid reason to pull Stan over in the first place.
option_e: Denied, because a search incident to a valid arrest in an automobile always includes the trunk of the automobile.
option_f: Denied, because the Officer had probable cause to search the trunk.
option_g: Denied, because the Officer had the right to search the car without a warrant.
option_h: Granted, because the trunk area was out of the range of Stan's control.
option_i: Denied, because the Officer was in the pursuit of his duties and the search was incidental to the arrest.
option_j: Granted, because the Officer did not observe Stan commit any crime.

question_text: Two years ago, a developer conveyed title to a lot to a woman by warranty deed. The woman purchased an owner's policy of title insurance before the an owner's policy of title insurance before the closing. Because of an error by the title insurance company, the title commitment and title policy failed to list a county tax lien encumbering the lot as an exception to coverage. Last year, the woman conveyed the lot for a bargain price by a quitclaim deed to a friend, who paid cash, did not conduct a title search, and did not purchase title insurance. Subsequently, the county began proceedings to foreclose the tax lien, and the friend filed a claim with the woman's title insurance company, demanding that the company reimburse her for the tax lien amount. Is the title insurance company obligated to pay the friend's claim?
option_a: No, because the friend did not conduct a title search before purchasing the lot.
option_b: No, because the friend did not purchase her own title insurance.
option_c: No, because the title insurance policy expired when the woman conveyed the lot to the friend.
option_d: Yes, because the tax lien was not listed as an exception on the title insurance policy.
option_e: No, because the woman no longer owns the property, and thus her title insurance is no longer valid.
option_f: Yes, because the woman used a quitclaim deed, which means all encumbrances should transfer to the new owner.
option_g: No, because the friend is not a named insured on the title insurance policy.
option_h: Yes, because the friend paid cash, making her eligible for claims.
option_i: Yes, because the woman is liable to the friend for breach of the covenant against encumbrances, and the title policy should cover the friend's potential loss.
option_j: Yes, because the title insurance company made an error in not listing the tax lien.

question_text: A defendant is on trial for kidnapping. The victim has testified that one of the kidnappers referred to the other as "Speed." The prosecutor calls a jail employee to testify that, while the defendant was in jail awaiting trial, other inmates addressed the defendant as "Speed." Is the jail employee's testimony admissible?
option_a: Yes, it's relevant to show the defendant's nickname.
option_b: No, because it's irrelevant to the case.
option_c: No, because the jail employee is not a reliable source of information.
option_d: Yes, to corroborate the truthfulness of the victim.
option_e: Yes, as circumstantial evidence that the defendant was one of the kidnappers.
option_f: No, because it is substantially more prejudicial than probative.
option_g: Yes, because it provides character evidence about the defendant.
option_h: No, because it's an opinion not based on the employee's personal knowledge.
option_i: Yes, because it's a statement made by the defendant.
option_j: No, because it is hearsay not within any exception. exception.

question_text: A defendant was arrested and prosecuted for the crime of false pretenses. At trial, the defendant testified that she prophesied the victim's imminent death. The defendant admitted that she told the victim she could save him if he brought a large sum of money wrapped in a handkerchief to a ritual. After the ritual, the defendant returned the handkerchief to the victim filled with waste paper rather than the money. After the defendant was convicted of false pretenses, she moved for a new trial and offered the affidavits of several jurors who sat on the case. An affidavit containing which of the following facts will most likely furnish the defendant's best basis for securing a new trial?
option_a: A juror was overheard discussing the details of the case outside the courtroom during the trial.
option_b: A juror admitted to having a personal financial interest in the outcome of the case.
option_c: A juror admittedly stated during deliberations that he had a personal animosity toward the defendant.
option_d: A juror admitted to having made a decision about the verdict before the trial began.
option_e: Ajuror fell asleep during defense counsel's closing argument.
option_f: A juror stated that she didn't believe in the concept of false pretenses, and therefore thought the defendant was not guilty from the start.
option_g: A juror confessed to having previously known the defendant but did not disclose this during jury selection.
option_h: A juror misunderstood the judge's instructions regarding the burden of proof.
option_i: A juror, in violation of the court's instructions, read a newspaper article implicating the defendant in several other similar schemes.

question_text: On January 1, a seller entered into an agreement of sale to convey his farm to a buyer. According to the contract, settlement (or closing) was to take place on March 1 of the same year. On January 20, a barn on the property was destroyed by fire. When the buyer was notified of the loss the following day, he contacted the seller and told him that the deal was off because of the fire. As a result, the buyer filed suit against the seller on January 25 for a refund of his down payment of $5,000. In his suit, the buyer will be unsuccessful and will be obliged to complete the contract if the court determines that on January 20
option_a: the buyer held the equitable title to the farm.
option_b: the buyer held the possessory title to the farm.
option_c: the buyer held the leasehold title to the farm.
option_d: the seller held the equitable title to the farm.
option_e: the seller held a conditional title to the farm.
option_f: the seller held the possessory title to the farm.
option_g: the buyer held the legal title to the farm.
option_h: the seller held the leasehold title to the farm.
option_i: the buyer held a conditional title to the farm.

question_text: A man brought a federal diversity action against his insurance company, alleging that the company had breached its duty under his insurance policy by refusing to pay for his medical expenses resulting from a mountain-biking accident. At the jury trial, the man presented evidence that he had paid all premiums on the insurance policy and that the policy covered personal-injury-related medical expenses arising from accidents. After he rested his case, the company presented evidence that a provision of the policy excluded payment for injury-related expenses resulting from an insured's "unduly risky" behavior. The company also presented a witness who testified that the accident had occurred in an area where posted signs warned bikers not to enter. The man did not cross-examine the witness. After resting its case, the company moved for judgment as a matter of law. Should the court grant the motion?
option_a: Yes, because the company's uncontradicted evidence of the man's unduly risky behavior means that no reasonable jury could find that the policy covers his injuries.
option_b: No, because the company did not provide sufficient evidence to prove that the man's behavior was unduly risky.
option_c: No, because the signs warning bikers not to enter were not part of the insurance policy.
option_d: No, because a motion for judgment as a matter of law must first be made at the close of the plaintiff's case-in-chief.
option_e: Yes, because the man failed to cross-examine the company's witness.
option_f: Yes, because the company provided evidence of a policy clause excluding unduly risky behavior.
option_g: Yes, because the man waived his right to rebut the company's evidence by not addressing the "unduly risky" policy provision in his case-in-chief.
option_h: No, because the man had paid all his premiums on the insurance policy.
option_i: No, because whether the man's behavior was unduly risky is a question of fact for the jury to resolve.
option_j: Yes, because the man did not provide sufficient evidence to prove that the company breached its duty.

question_text: The defendant has been charged with murder in the killing of his wife. The defense attorney is preparing a defense to show facts sufficient to support a verdict of voluntary manslaughter. Counsel has to prove all of the necessary elements during trial in order to support a voluntary manslaughter charge to the jury. Which of the following would not be proper proof to establish a voluntary manslaughter charge to the jury?
option_a: The defendant acted without deliberation and premeditation.
option_c: The defendant was provoked by accidentally seeing his wife having sexual relations with his best friend.
option_d: The defendant was provoked by his wife's refusal to prepare his favorite meal.
option_e: The defendant acted in an overpowering heat of passion.
option_f: The defendant acted several days after the provoking events.
option_g: The defendant was provoked by a minor disagreement with his wife.
option_h: The defendant acted while under the influence of alcohol or drugs.

question_text: Defendant was waiting in line for post-Thanksgiving day sales. The door opened at 6 a.m. and hundreds of people rushed the entrance for a chance at a hugely discounted holiday gift. When defendant saw that there was a signed copy of a book written by his mother's favorite author, he jumped towards it, only to be blocked by a little old lady with a baseball bat. She was using the bat as a blocking tool, but he was determined to get the book, so he deliberately stuck his leg out in front of her and tripped her. She fell hard and in the process ended up with a blood clot in her leg, which traveled to her heart over a period of weeks, and ultimately caused her death. What crime can the defendant likely be convicted of?
option_h: No crime, it was a accident.

question_text: A landowner was the record owner of a 30-acre orchard outside the city. The landowner lived in a farmhouse on the orchard and used the property to raise produce. Adjoining the orchard directly to the west was a 10-acre vineyard that was owned in fee simple by a farmer. A four-lane highway adjoined the orchard directly to the east. The farmer discovered that the southern portion of the orchard was rarely used by the landowner for any of the landowner's farming activities and found a convenient gravel road leading from the vineyard across the orchard all the way to the four- lane highway. The farmer used this road adversely and openly for the entire 20-year prescriptive period, and in doing so, the farmer has acquired a prescriptive easement over the roadway across the orchard. Thereafter, the farmer conveys the vineyard to a buyer in fee simple. The deed recited that "the grantor hereby conveys the vineyard, together with an easement for the right of way across the orcharX, to the grantee. "After the buyer took possession of the vineyard, the landowner brought an appropriate action to prevent him from using the roadway across the orchard. The issue that will determine the outcome of this suit is whether
option_b: the roadway was necessary for the farmer's use of the vineyard.
option_c: easements can be reserved to third parties.
option_d: the buyer had knowledge of the easement prior to purchasing the vineyard.
option_e: the description in the farmer's deed to the buyer was adequate to identify the portion of the orchard that the farmer used as a roadway.
option_f: the farmer had the right to transfer the easement to the buyer.
option_g: the landowner had given express permission for the roadway to be used.
option_h: the buyer will make excessive use of the roadway.
option_i: the farmer used the roadway continuously for the required prescriptive period.
option_j: the deed mentioned the easement specifically.

question_text: A foreign visitor was on trial for kidnapping a small child. The prosecutor stated that the visitor knew the child personally, which is why the child went with him, and that the perpetrator knew the child's parents had money. The prosecutor called a witness to testify that the perpetrator told the witness "I am looking forward to visiting with the child and his parents. They have a wonderfully luxurious house that I will enjoy staying in.'' The defense objected to the proposed testimony. Will the court likely sustain the objection?
option_a: Yes, the statement does not directly implicate the accused in the crime.
option_b: No, the statement will serve to corroborate the prosecutor's theory of the case.
option_c: Yes, the statement is irrelevant to the issue of guilt or innocence.
option_d: Yes, the statement should be excluded because it could bias the jury.
option_e: Yes, the prejudice of the statement will greatly outweigh its probative value.
option_f: No, the statement can come in as impeachment if the accused takes the stand, and as a party admission to show the material facts of knowing the child and that the family had money.
option_g: Yes, the statement is hearsay and therefore inadmissible.
option_h: No, the statement helps establish the relationship between the accused and the child.
option_i: No, the statement is admissible to impeach the accused and establish he is lying on cross, if he takes the stand.

question_text: A manufacturer sued a buyer in federal court for failing to make timely payments under the parties' sales contract. The case was tried to the court solely on documentary evidence. Immediately after the close of the evidence, the judge announced from the bench, "Judgment shall be entered for the manufacturer," and judgment was so entered. The buyer has appealed the judgment. What is the buyer's best argument for persuading the appellate court to reverse the judgment?
option_a: The buyer was not given sufficient time to prepare a defense.
option_b: The trial court erred by making a decision without hearing oral arguments.
option_c: The manufacturer was required to file proposed findings and conclusions before the trial court ruled.
option_d: The trial court erred because it did not consider the buyer's financial circumstances.
option_e: The trial court erred by not providing findings and conclusions.
option_f: The judgment is clearly erroneous because it was based solely on documentary evidence.
option_g: The trial court erred by not allowing the buyer to cross-examine the manufacturer.
option_h: The judgment is invalid because the trial court did not consider the credibility of the documentary evidence.
option_i: The manufacturer failed to establish a prima facie case against the buyer.

question_text: A man is charged with murder. During the trial, defense counsel offers the testimony of a man on death row admitting he hired another man to shoot the victim. The testimony is
option_a: hearsay, but admissible as an admission.
option_b: hearsay, but admissible as a dying declaration.
option_c: hearsay, but admissible as a prior statement by witness.
option_d: hearsay not within any recognized exception.
option_f: hearsay, but admissible as a statement under belief of impending death.
option_g: hearsay, but admissible as a statement made during or immediately after the event.
option_h: hearsay, but admissible as a statement made for medical diagnosis or treatment.
option_i: hearsay, but admissible as a statement of personal or family history.

question_text: A defendant was going out to dinner, and she parked her car at a parking lot across the street from the restaurant. The parking attendant directed the defendant to leave her keys in the car, and he handed her a receipt as she left. Following dinner, the defendant went back to the parking lot and handed her receipt to the attendant who informed her that the parking charge would be $14. Visibly irate, she told the attendant that $14 was much too high for a two-hour parking fee. The parking attendant responded that unless she paid the $14, he could not return her car. She adamantly refused to pay the $14, and her friend, with whom she was having dinner, drove the defendant home in her car. Later that same night, the defendant decided to regain possession of her car with a spare set of keys. She returned to the lot and drove her car home. If the defendant is subsequently charged with larceny, she will most likely be found
option_a: not guilty, because she did not take possession of another person's property.
option_b: guilty, because she intentionally avoided paying the parking fee by using her spare keys.
option_c: guilty, because she acted with the intent to deprive the parking lot of its rightful fee.
option_d: guilty, because she was under a binding contractual obligation to pay the parking fee.
option_e: not guilty, because the parking attendant did not have a legal right to withhold her car.
option_f: not guilty, because the parking lot's charges were not agreed upon in advance.
option_g: not guilty, because the $14 charge was excessively high.
option_h: guilty, because she failed to pay the $14 before regaining possession of her car.
option_i: guilty, because she knowingly took her car without the consent of the parking attendant.
option_j: not guilty, because the defendant cannot be charged with larceny of her own motor vehicle.

question_text: A doctor is charged with the murder of his wife. The prosecution alleges that he murdered his wife by giving her a massive injection of succinylcholine while she was asleep. Succinylcholine is a drug used in small quantities by anesthesiologists as a muscle relaxant. The prosecution claims that the fatal dose given to the wife so totally relaxed her lung muscles that she suffocated. During the trial, a toxicologist, who had no previous knowledge of the case, sat in court and heard all of the evidence about the wife's death and autopsy. As part of the doctor's defense, his attorney calls the toxicologist to give his opinion on the cause of the wife's death. May the toxicologist so testify?
option_a: No, because he did not directly examine the wife's body.
option_b: No, because the cause of death is an issue to be decided by the jury.
option_c: Yes, because an expert may base his opinion on facts made known to him at the trial.
option_d: Yes, because he has relevant experience in the field of toxicology.
