option_d: $12,500, or half the commission, because the sale reached the halfway point when the nephew and the developer signed the contract.
option_e: Nothing, because as a third-party beneficiary of the contract between the nephew and the developer, the agent can enforce the contract only against the developer, but not against the nephew.
option_f: $25,000, or the commission equivalent of 5 percent on the sale of the property for$500,000, because fulfillment of the consummation condition was prevented by an event beyond the agent's control.
option_g: $25,000, because the agent provided a buyer and thus fulfilled his part of the contract.
option_h: Nothing, because the sale did not go through and hence no commission is due.
option_i: Nothing, because the consummation condition has not been fulfilled, and the nephew's refusal to sue the developer will not excuse that condition.
option_j: $25,000, because the agent upheld his end of the contract in making his best efforts to find a buyer for the parcel.

question_text: On the basis of scientific studies showing a causal relationship between the consumption of "red meat" (principally beef) and certain forms of cancer, a federal statute prohibits all commercial advertising of red meat products. The statute does not, however, restrict the sale of red meat products. Producers of red meat have challenged the statute as a violation of their free speech rights protected by the First Amendment. Is the court likely to find the statute constitutional?
option_a: Yes, because it is directly related to the government's interest in preventing health risks.
option_b: No, because it unfairly targets a specific industry.
option_c: Yes, because it only restricts commercial advertising, not personal speech.
option_d: No, because it is more extensive than necessary to serve the government interest in preventing certain cancers.
option_e: Yes, because it restricts potentially harmful information from reaching the public.
option_f: No, because it does not provide an alternative method for meat producers to advertise their products.
option_g: No, because it does not serve a substantial government interest.
option_h: Yes, because it serves a legitimate government interest in protecting public health.
option_i: Yes, because it does not affect speech protected by the First Amendment.
option_j: No, because it violates the producers' right to commercial speech.

question_text: Question 13. A buyer sent a signed letter to a seller that stated: "Ship 100 boxes of nails at $3 per box, the price quoted in your circular." The seller mailed the buyer a signed form acknowledgment that agreed to the buyer's terms and stated on the reverse side: "Disputes regarding quality shall be arbitrated." The buyer did not reply to the seller's acknowledgment, and the seller shipped the nails. When the buyer received the nails, it found their quality to be unsatisfactory and sued the seller for breach of warranty. The seller has asked an attorney whether the parties' contract requires arbitration of the buyer's claim. What is the best advice the attorney can provide?
option_a: A contract was formed when the buyer received the nails, but the arbitration term is not valid as it was not explicitly agreed upon by the buyer.
option_b: A contract was formed pursuant to conduct when the buyer received the nails, and a court would exclude the arbitration provision from the contract.
option_c: A contract was formed when the seller mailed its acknowledgment, and the arbitration term must be accepted by the buyer because it was included in the acknowledgment.
option_d: No contract exists, because the arbitration term in the seller's acknowledgment was a new term that was not in the original agreement.
option_e: No contract exists, because the arbitration term in the seller's acknowledgment created a counteroffer that the buyer never accepted.
option_f: A contract was formed when the buyer sent the letter, and the arbitration term is not valid because the buyer didn't agree to it.
option_g: A contract was formed when the seller mailed its acknowledgment, and the court must decide whether the arbitration term should be excluded as a material alteration of the contract.
option_h: A contract was formed when the seller mailed its acknowledgment, and the arbitration term automatically became part of the contract because the buyer didn't object.
option_i: A contract was formed when the seller mailed its acknowledgment, and the arbitration term became part of the contract. arbitration term became part of the contract.
option_j: No contract exists, because the buyer didn't respond to the seller's acknowledgment.

question_text: A construction company was doing repairs and replacing portions of a sidewalk and railing next to a lake. The construction crew started tearing out the old sidewalk and railing, but stopped work when it started to get dark. The construction crew left without putting up a warning sign or barrier around the work area. A few hours later, a jogger came along the sidewalk. Not realizing the construction work was in progress there, the jogger stumbled and fell at the spot where the construction crew had torn up the sidewalk and railing. The jogger fell into the lake. As the jogger was attempting to stay afloat, he began screaming, "Help! Help! I can't swim. I'm drowning. " His screams attracted the attention of a person who was passing on his bicycle. The cyclist immediately hurried to assist the jogger. As the cyclist was leaning over the edge of the lake, trying to help the jogger get out of the water, he lost his balance and fell into the lake. Both the jogger and cyclist suffered serious bodily injuries before they were pulled out of the water by police. In a negligence action by the cyclist to recover for his personal injuries, the construction company will most probably
option_a: not be held liable, because the construction company could not foresee that anyone would be hurt while trying to rescue someone from the lake.
option_b: not be held liable, because the cyclist assumed the risk by leaning over the edge of the lake.
option_c: not be held liable, because it was the jogger's responsibility to avoid the construction area.
option_d: be held liable, because they did not properly secure the worksite after finishing for the day.
option_e: be held liable, because the construction company would be strictly liable to anyone injured by the failure to put adequate warnings or barriers around the site of the sidewalk repairs.
option_f: be held liable, because the cyclist's attempt to rescue the jogger was foreseeable.
option_g: not be held liable, because the jogger should have been aware of the construction work.
option_h: be held liable, because they failed to provide adequate lighting for the construction site.
option_i: not be held liable, because the cyclist voluntarily put himself in danger.

question_text: A women's action group attempted for many months, unsuccessfully, to reach an agreement with the local professional men's club to admit women to membership. The women's group instituted a suit for a declaratory judgment in federal court to determine whether the men's club was subject to the state's anti-discrimination act. Prior to the elections for city officials, four members of the women's group were sent to picket the offices of the mayor and district attorney, both prominent members of the men's club. Two members walked outside the front of the mayor's office building, carrying signs that read, "The mayor is supposed to serve all the people but his lunch club is for men ONLY. So don't vote for him. " The other two pickets walked outside the rear of the district attorney's office building, carrying similar signs, telling the public not to vote for him. This picketing was carried on from 9 A. M. to 5 P. M. The same day, two more pickets were assigned to carry identical signs in front of the mayor's official residence. Two pickets also carried duplicate signs in front of the district attorney's suburban home during the early evening hours. The picketing at all sites was held peacefully without any disturbance. The relevant city ordinances concerning picketing read as follows:"Section 201. No picketing shall be permitted inside of, or on any sidewalk or street immediately adjacent or contiguous to, city hall, without express permission of the mayor. Applications for such permission shall be filed at least three days before such picketing is intended to begin and shall state the purpose, place, and time of the proposed picketing. Section 202. It shall be unlawful for any person to engage in picketing before or about the residence of an individual. Nothing herein shall be deemed to prohibit the holding of a meeting or assembly on any premises used for the discussion of subjects of general public interest. "The federal district court will most likely avoid making a decision on the merits of the suit for declaratory judgment because
option_b: the women's group has not exhausted all other legal remedies.
option_c: the federal court does not have jurisdiction over state laws.
option_d: the men's club is not a public entity.
option_e: the women's group did not obtain the mayor's permission for picketing.
option_f: the picketing does not violate the city ordinances.
option_g: the relief sought is essentially for an advisory opinion.
option_h: there is no case or controversy.
option_j: the picketing was peaceful and did not cause any disturbance.

question_text: A ski resort had a lift that carried customers from the bottom to the top of the ski slope. One afternoon, the lift suddenly broke down because of a power failure in the area. A customer was suspended in his lift chair, one-third of the way up the hill and 50 feet above the ground. The customer remained on the lift for five hours until power was restored. He was then returned uninjured to the bottom of the hill. In a suit against the ski resort, the customer is likely to have action for

A defendant was charged with felony murder as a result of his setting fire to the victim's house. The victim was upstairs sleeping when the fire was set, and he died in his bedroom during the fire. If the defendant can prove the facts to support his defense, which of the following assertions would LEAST likely remove liability for felony murder?
option_a: The defendant was coerced by another to set the fire.
option_b: The defendant did not intend to kill the victim.
option_c: The defendant thought the house was empty when he set the fire.
option_d: The defendant believed the victim had already evacuated the house.
option_e: The defendant was insane when he set the fire.
option_f: The victim died of a heart attack before the fire spread to his bedroom.

question_text: A company got a building permit to construct a total of 300 apartment units to be contained in three high-rise buildings on its 7-acre property. A group of adjoining landowners who owned single-family residences sued for an injunction, alleging various environmental violations. The developer did not have a significant investment at that point, and decided to stop the construction.Shortly thereafter, the city passed a comprehensive new citywide zoning law that re-zoned the subject property and some large tracts of adjoining land to low-density residential, allowing one residence for each 4.6 acres. This destroyed the multi-unit plan and the city cancelled the permit, which the developer did not appeal.The developer's land value decreased from $2 million to $100,000.The developer sued the city in federal court on the basis that this was illegal spot zoning constituting a taking in violation of due process.Will the developer prevail with his federal constitutional claim?
option_a: Yes, the city cannot change zoning laws after issuing a building permit without compensating the developer.
option_b: No, the developer was aware of potential zoning changes and should have taken that into consideration.
option_c: No, the developer should have made a significant investment and appealed the city's decision.
option_d: Yes, because he got a building permit and justifiably relied on that approval  the rezoning was an illegal act once it granted a building permit.
option_e: Yes, the developer had a right to construct the planned units as per the original permit.
option_f: Yes, because the new zoning law disproportionately affected the developer compared to other landowners.
option_g: No, the city has the right to rezone land for the greater good of the community.
option_h: Yes, because the developer's loss was caused by discriminatory spot zoning that took its property without due process.
option_i: Yes, the state cannot cause such a dramatic diminution in an owner's property without paying just compensation.
option_j: No, this was a comprehensive new zoning plan that rezoned the entire city; the developer shared the loss with the adjoining owners who were also rezoned.

question_text: In 1973, a woman was the actual and record owner of 20 acres of certain undeveloped timberland. In September 1973, the woman mortgaged the 20 acres of timberland to the bank by a mortgage deed (in the traditional form of a conveyance in fee simple subject to a condition subsequent), which was not recorded until January 1974. The mortgage deed contained the following clause immediately after the legal description of the 20 acres of timberland:"Together with all the real property now owned by (the woman) or which shall be owned by (the woman) during the continuance of the indebtedness secured by this mortgage. " This mortgage was given, as the recorded instrument revealed, to secure a note for $100,000 repayable over a 40-year term. In March 2004, the woman, using money loaned by a finance company, purchased a 50-acre mountainous estate situated outside of a major city. This deed was recorded immediately. In April 2004, the woman executed and delivered to the finance company a mortgage to secure its loan. This mortgage was promptly and duly recorded. The finance company had no actual notice of the prior mortgage to the bank. In February 2007, the woman defaulted on both mortgages. The bank then initiated foreclosure proceedings against the woman and the finance company as joint defendants. In its foreclosure action, the bank averred that its mortgage was a first mortgage on both the 20 acres of timberland and the 50-acre estate. The finance company filed a cross-complaint, alleging that its mortgage was a first mortgage on the 50-acre estate and praying for foreclosure on that parcel of property. In order to decide this case in favor of the bank, the court does not need to resolve which of the following issues?
option_a: Whether the bank's mortgage deed was legally recorded.
option_b: Whether the finance company is charged with record notice of the bank's mortgage.
option_c: Whether the finance company can rely on the doctrine of implied purchase money liens.
option_d: Whether the finance company's mortgage is a purchase money mortgage.
option_e: Whether the quoted clause in the mortgage instrument to the 20 acres of timberland covers the 50-acre estate.
option_f: Whether the woman's default on both mortgages is legally valid.
option_g: Whether the woman's purchase of the 50-acre estate was legally valid.
option_h: Whether the bank's foreclosure proceedings were initiated in a timely manner.
option_i: Whether the finance company had actual knowledge of the woman's mortgage with the bank.

question_text: A state highway runs through the center of a city's business district. As the highway passes through this business district, there is a stretch where the highway is too narrow to satisfS' the safety standards for state roadways. Along this narrow stretch and on opposite sides of the street are located two businesses. Situated on the east side of the street is a bar and grill; directly across the street on the west side is a lounge. In order to comply with state highway regulations, the state highway department, acting pursuant to state statute, ordered the city to prohibit parking on one side of the highway within the business district. The city council convened a special meeting to determine whether parking should be prohibited on the east or west side of the street. During the meeting, the council was unable to resolve the issue. Finally, the city council president decided the only fair way to resolve the matter was to flip a coin. After the coin flip, the city prohibited parking at all times on the west side of the highway. On account of this decision, parking in front of the lounge was permanently prohibited. This caused the lounge to suffer a substantial decline in business because of the unavailability of on-street parking. As a consequence, the owner of the lounge brought suit for declarative relief against the city alleging that the parking ban on his side of the street was unconstitutional. In all likelihood, will the plaintiff be successful in this cause of action?
option_a: No, because as a governmental entity, the city enjoys immunity from such suits under the Eleventh Amendment.
option_b: No, because the city was acting in accordance with state highway safety regulations.
option_c: Yes, because the action of the city violated the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
option_d: No, because the city's action was reasonable.
option_e: Yes, because the city council failed to consider the economic impact of their decision on the lounge.
option_f: No, because the city council president's decision to flip a coin was arbitrary and capricious.
option_g: No, because the state highway department's order supersedes any claim by the lounge owner.
option_h: Yes, because the prohibition of parking constitutes a taking of property without just compensation.
option_i: Yes, because the city council's decision unfairly targeted businesses on the west side of the street.

question_text: A city ordinance forbids washing vehicles parked on city streets. A man washes his car while it is parked on the street in front of his home. The water forms a puddle in the street, which freezes over during the night. Early the next morning, a car driving on the street slides on the ice and crashes into a tree. If the driver of the car sues the man and asserts that the man was negligent per Se, which of the following additional facts would help the man the most?
option_a: The man had already planned to dry the water before it froze, but was unable to because of an unexpected emergency.
option_b: The driver lives in the man's neighborhood and saw the man washing his car the day before the accident.
option_c: The city had been warned about the icy conditions but had not salted the streets.
option_d: The driver is not a resident of the city.
option_e: The man had been washing his car on the street for years without any previous incidents.
option_f: The driver had previously been involved in multiple accidents due to reckless driving.
option_g: The man had placed a warning sign near the puddle to alert drivers of the potential hazard.
option_h: The man was not aware of the ordinance.
option_i: The driver was driving at an excessively high speed at the time of the accident.
option_j: The city council enacted the ordinance after someone washing his car was hit by a passing truck.

question_text: A seller contracted to manufacture 1,000 toasters for a buyer for a specified price. The contract contained a provision that clearly stated: "This contract may not be assigned, and any violation of this prohibition voids the contract." After the contract was signed, the seller informed the buyer that the toasters would be manufactured by a competitor of the seller. Citing the non-assignment provision, the buyer claimed that it was no longer bound by the contract. Toasters manufactured by the competitor were of equal quality to toasters manufactured by the seller. Is the buyer bound by the contract?
option_a: Yes, because even though the seller breached the contract, there are no damages since the competitor's toasters are of equal quality to the seller's toasters.
option_b: No, because the seller assigned a right despite the contractual prohibition.
option_c: Yes, because the quality of the toasters did not change even though the manufacturer did.
option_d: No, because the seller violated the contractual prohibition by delegating its duty to a competitor.
option_e: No, because "this contract may not be assigned" means that duties may not be delegated, and the seller delegated a duty.
option_f: No, because the non-assignment clause voids the contract when violated.
option_g: No, because the competitor is not a party to the original contract.
option_h: Yes, because the seller informed the buyer about the change in manufacturer.
option_i: Yes, because the non-assignment provision is not enforceable since public policy favors free assignment and delegation.
option_j: Yes, because the buyer did not explicitly reject the toasters manufactured by the competitor.

question_text: A builder wanted to have security systems installed in a series of homes he was building. He contacted several companies and asked them to submit bids for the installation work. An alarm company decided to submit a bid and, in turn, requested bids from several wholesalers for the burglar alarms it planned to use if it was awarded the job. A supplier submitted a bid to the alarm company that the latter used in computing the bid that it was preparing for the builder. On September 1, the alarm company sent the builder its bid, in which it proposed to install the security systems for $100,000. On September 9, the supplier notified the alarm company that it would be unable to supply any burglar alarms to them. On September 11, the builder sent the following fax to the alarm company: "I hereby accept your offer to install the security systems for $100,000." The alarm company had to pay another wholesaler $10,000 above the price quoted by the supplier for the burglar alarms. As a result, the alarm company advised the builder that the total price for the job would have to be increased to $10,000. The builder replied that he would hold the alarm company to the initially agreed price of $100,000. The alarm company installed the security systems, but the builder has not yet paid them anything. In an action by the alarm company against the builder for services rendered, the alarm company will probably be able to recover
option_a: The cost of the alarms from the second supplier, because they were an unforeseen expense.
option_b: $110,000 because of an unanticipated change of circumstances after the parties had entered into their contract.
option_c: only in quantum meruit, because by demanding $110,000 the alarm company repudiated its contract with the builder.
option_d: only in quantum meruit, because of the doctrine of commercial frustration.
option_e: $100,000, because that was the original agreed price, regardless of changes in circumstances.
option_f: $90,000, because the alarm company had to pay an additional cost.
option_g: $100,000, because that was the contract price.
option_h: Nothing, because the builder did not agree to the new price.
option_i: $100,000, plus damages for the builder's delay in payment.
option_j: $110,000, because the supplier's inability to supply burglar alarms was unforeseen.

question_text: Four years ago the owner of a shopping center leased a store in the center for a 10-year term to a pharmacist for use as a drugstore. The pharmacist established and operated a drugstore at the leased premises. The lease included provisions that described the shopping center by metes and bounds; identified the entrances, parking areas, signs, and other common facilities of the shopping center; and set out a covenant that the owner would not lease any part of the shopping center to another drugstore. Last year the owner purchased a parcel of land immediately adjacent to the shopping center. That parcel was improved with a building that, at the time of the owner's purchase and for ten years earlier, was occupied in part by a food supermarket and in part by a discount drugstore, under leases which the prior owner assigned to the owner. The owner reconstructed the common facilities of both shopping centers to integrate them and combine them so that, to the public, the two centers appeared as a larger single development. The pharmacist learned that the lease of the discount drugstore was about to expire and that the owner planned to enter into a new lease of the same space with the discount drugstore. The pharmacist protested the proposed new lease, but the owner declared his intention to go forward with it. The pharmacist brought an appropriate action to enjoin the new lease to the discount drugstore as a violation of the covenant in the pharmacist's lease. If the court finds for the owner, what will be the likely reason?
option_a: The covenant does not apply to the owner's actions as he did not directly lease the space to another drugstore but bought a property which already had a lease with a drugstore.
option_b: The owner's purchase and integration of the adjacent parcel of land effectively nullifies the existing covenant.
option_c: The drugstore use on the adjacent parcel was in existence when the owner and the pharmacist first entered into the lease.
option_d: The discount drugstore on the adjacent parcel is not considered a 'new' lease, but a continuation of an existing one.
option_e: A covenant cannot restrict the use of land not owned by the covenantor when the covenant was created.
option_f: The owner has the right to lease any part of his property to any business, regardless of existing leases.
option_g: The pharmacist did not adequately express his concerns to the owner before initiating legal action.
option_h: A covenant that attempts to restrict competition is invalid as against public policy even if it runs with the land.
option_i: The covenant in the pharmacist's lease can be fairly construed as to apply only to the original shopping center premises.
option_j: The covenant is not applicable as the shopping centers were considered separate entities at the time of the lease agreement.

question_text: The attorney for a plaintiff in an action filed in federal district court served the defendant with the summons, the complaint, and 25 interrogatories asking questions about the defendant's contentions in the case. The interrogatories stated that they were to be answered within 30 days after service. The defendant is likely to succeed in obtaining a protective order on which of the following grounds?
option_a: The interrogatories were not accompanied by a summons and complaint.
option_b: The defendant is not required to respond to interrogatories during a federal district court action.
option_c: Interrogatories may not be served until an answer to the complaint is filed.
option_d: The interrogatories exceed the number permitted without permission from the court or an agreement between the parties.
option_e: The defendant is not required to respond to the interrogatories within 30 days.
option_f: The interrogatories contain questions that are excessively burdensome or expensive to answer.
option_g: The interrogatories were not properly served to the defendant.
option_h: The interrogatories contain questions that are irrelevant to the case.
option_i: Interrogatories may not be served until the parties have conferred to arrange for initial disclosures and prepare a discovery plan.
option_j: Interrogatories are only proper to discover facts, not contentions.

question_text: A purchasing agent for a women's clothing store negotiated a contract with dressmaking company to purchase a specified quantity of khaki garments at a price of $75,000. One week later, the purchasing agent received a telephone call from the vice president of the dressmaking company, who informed her that the dressmaking company's sales representative had made an error in calculating the contract price. As a result, the vice president said that unless the women's clothing store agreed to pay an additional $15,000, the garments would not be delivered. If the purchasing agent should have known that the dressmaking company's original price term of $75,000 was in error, but agreed to the contract anyway, which of the following is the most accurate statement?
option_a: There was an enforceable contract at the original price term, because the purchasing agent acted in good faith.
option_b: There was a voidable contract, because the purchasing agent should have known of the error.
option_c: There was no valid contract formed, because there was no mutuality of assent.
option_d: There was no valid contract formed, because the price was not agreed upon by both parties.
option_e: There was a valid contract formed, because the dressmaking company was responsible for their own error.
option_f: The contract is invalid, because the purchasing agent took advantage of the dressmaking company's error.
option_g: There was an enforceable contract at the revised price term, because the error was corrected before delivery.
option_h: There was an enforceable contract at the original price term, because the mistake was unilateral.
option_i: There was a voidable contract, because the dressmaking company made an error.
option_j: There was an enforceable contract at the original price term, because the mistake resulted from an error in computation, not in judgment.

question_text: In transactions between merchants, what is not true about additional terms proposed in the acceptance?
option_a: They become part of the contract only if both parties are merchants.
option_b: They become part of the contract only if the offeree does not object within a reasonable time.
option_c: They become part of the contract only if the offeree agrees to them within a reasonable time.
option_d: Additional terms always become part of the contract.
option_e: They become part of the contract only if the additional terms are in writing.
option_f: They become part of the contract unless they materially alter the agreement.
option_g: They become part of the contract only if the offeror sends a written confirmation within a reasonable time.
option_h: They never become part of the contract.
option_i: They become part of the contract unless the offer expressly limits acceptance to the terms of the offer.
option_j: They become part of the contract unless the offeror objects within a reasonable time to the additional terms.

question_text: A defendant, who was an indigent, was arrested and charged with possession of less than one ounce of marijuana. Under the relevant state statute, possession of less than one ounce of marijuana is a misdemeanor, punishable by a maximum of one year in jail and/or a fine of $500. At trial, the defendant pleaded not guilty and requested that an attorney be appointed to represent him. The trial judge refused to honor the defendant's request for an attorney. If the defendant is subsequently found guilty, which of the following is the most severe sentence that can constitutionally be imposed?
option_a: One year in jail, but with the possibility of parole after six months.
option_c: Three months in jail and a $250 fine.
option_d: Probation for one year and a $500 fine.

question_text: A man was driving his car when he suddenly hit a pedestrian as she was crossing the street. Following the accident, an ambulance was summoned and the pedestrian was taken to a nearby hospital. The man, who also drove to the hospital, approached the pedestrian in the emergency room while she was being treated for her injuries. When the pedestrian saw the man, she told him, "You went through that red light. You could have killed me. " She then asked the man for $5,000 to cover her anticipated medical expenses. The man replied that he would have to think about it. Shortly thereafter, the man sent a letter to the pedestrian in which he wrote, "I realize I drove through the red light. Naturally, I would like to settle this without any litigation. I'm willing to pay all your medical expenses and give you $2,000 if you don't sue me. " The pedestrian did not accept the man's offer and brought suit against him. At trial, the pedestrian's attorney offers the first sentence of the letter into evidence, and the man objects. The trial judge should rule the evidence
option_a: inadmissible as an admission made in connection with settlement negotiations.
option_b: inadmissible, because the man was not under oath when he wrote the letter.
option_c: inadmissible, as the pedestrian did not accept the man's offer.
option_d: inadmissible as it infringes on the man's privacy rights.
option_e: admissible as it is a written document.
option_f: admissible as it was not part of formal litigation.
option_g: inadmissible as an admission made in connection with an offer to pay medical expenses.
option_i: inadmissible, because documents must be offered in their entirety.
option_j: admissible as it is a direct confession of the man's guilt.

question_text: A state labor code provided that no employer in the state shall knowingly employ an alien who is not lawfully residing in the United States if such employment would have an adverse effect on lawful residents' right to work. A group of immigrant farm workers were continually refused employment contracts by the labor contractors in the state. Instead, the labor contractors had employed many alien workers since they would toil longer hours for less wages. The immigrant farm workers now bring suit in state court pursuant to the above-mentioned statutory provision. Which of the following determinations would most likely result as a consequence of the immigrant farm workers' lawsuit?
option_a: The court would declare the statute unconstitutional as violative of the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
option_b: The court would declare the statute unconstitutional as it violates the rights of the alien workers.
option_c: The court would declare the statute constitutional since it is within the state's power to regulate employment.
option_d: The court would declare the statute constitutional as within the realm of rights reserved to the states by the Eleventh Amendment.
option_e: The court would declare the statute unconstitutional as it discriminates against non-residents.
option_f: The court would declare the statute unconstitutional as it conflicts with federal labor laws.
option_g: The court would declare the statute constitutional as it does not infringe on the rights of the immigrant workers.
option_h: The court would declare the statute constitutional as it promotes the welfare of the state's residents.
option_i: The court would declare the statute constitutional since the states are not preempted in the area of economic regulation of illegal aliens.

question_text: Each year, the local boys' and girls' club sponsors a "Baseball Card Bonanza. " During the three- day event, collectors of baseball cards gather, exchanging and swapping old cards. A boy and a girl, both 12-year-old youngsters at the bonanza, were looking at each other's cards when they were approached by a 32-year-old collector. The collector asked the boy and girl if he could look through their baseball cards. The boy and girl nodded affirmatively. As the collector was flipping through the boy's cards, he found a 1948 card featuring Branch Rickey, the former owner of the Brooklyn Dodgers. The collector, who knew the card was worth more than $500, offered the boy 50 cents for the card. The boy thought the card had very little value because he knew that Rickey was not a major league baseball player. The boy accepted the 50 cents and gave the Rickey baseball card to the collector. The next day the boy found out that the baseball card was worth$600. If the boy asserts a claim against the collector for deceit, will the boy prevail?
option_a: Yes, because the boy was the true owner of the card and was entitled to the benefit of the bargain.
option_b: Yes, because the collector used his knowledge of the card's value to exploit the boy.
option_c: Yes, because the collector did not disclose the true value of the card.
option_d: Yes, because the collector took advantage of the boy's lack of knowledge about the card's value.
option_e: No, because the collector made no false representations of fact.
option_f: No, because the boy willingly accepted the offer made by the collector.
option_g: Yes, because the collector was obligated to disclose the true value of the card.
option_h: No, because the boy was not justified in relying on the collector's offer.
option_i: No, because the boy had the opportunity to decline the collector's offer.
option_j: No, because the collector didn't force the boy to sell the card.

question_text: Donny threw a knife at Sally, intending to injure her severely. However, Donny missed Sally. Sally saw the knife just as it whizzed by her head, missing it by about one inch. As a result, Sally was very scared. Sally sued Donny for assault and battery. Which of the following is most correct?
option_a: Donny will be liable for battery, but not assault.
option_b: Donny will not be liable for either assault or battery because he did not actually hit Sally with the knife.
option_c: Donny will be liable for assault, but not battery because he missed Sally with the knife.
option_d: Donny will be liable for assault and for battery.
option_e: Donny will not be liable for either assault or battery because this is only a criminal matter.
option_f: Donny will not be liable for either assault or battery because Sally was not physically harmed.
option_g: Donny will be liable for assault and battery because he intended to cause harm.
option_h: Donny will be liable for both assault and battery because Sally was terrified.
option_i: Donny will be liable for battery, but not assault because Sally was able to dodge the knife.
option_j: Donny will be liable for assault, but not battery.

question_text: Hume's attack on natural law is founded on his argument that:
option_a: There is no universal agreement on what constitutes natural law.
option_b: Natural law is an arbitrary concept.
option_c: Natural law does not apply to all cultures and societies.
option_e: There is no such thing as a social contract.
option_f: We cannot objectively know what is morally right or wrong.
option_g: Natural law is an outdated concept.
option_h: Natural law is not based on empirical evidence.
option_i: Natural law fails to protect the state against attacks.
option_j: Natural law does not give adequate consideration to individual rights.

question_text: Which statement is closest to characterizing the principal difference between the positions adopted by Hobbes and Locke?
option_a: They differ in their views about the necessity of a social contract.
option_b: They disagree about the role of law in society.
option_c: They have contrasting views on the role of religion in society.
option_d: They have different ideas about the importance of education.
option_e: They have different perspectives on economic policies.
option_f: They have opposing views about the nature of contractual obligations.
option_g: They differ in respect of their account of life before the social contract.
option_h: They hold opposing views on the subject of individual rights.
option_i: They disagree on the structure and role of government in society.
option_j: They adopt different attitudes towards the role of the courts in maintaining order.

question_text: In which of the following the idea of ‘natural law’ first appeared
Which of these statements best describes the UK Constitution?
option_a: The UK Constitution is a direct copy of the US Constitution
option_b: The UK Constitution is codified and contained in a single document
option_c: The UK Constitution can be changed by a simple majority vote in Parliament
option_d: The UK Constitution gives the judiciary the power to overturn acts of parliament
option_e: The UK Constitution is based on a Bill of Rights
option_f: The UK Constitution's power is derived solely from Parliament
option_g: The UK Constitution is based entirely on European Union law
option_h: The UK Constitution is uncodified and can be found in a number of sources
option_i: The UK Constitution's only source of power is that of the sovereign
option_j: The UK Constitution is based solely on common law

question_text: What is the fundamental prerequisite for the incorporation of custom under English law?
option_a: Custom is superior to English law and is always incorporated
option_b: Custom is not incorporated under English law
option_c: Custom is incorporated if it has been approved by the Queen
option_d: Custom is incorporated if it is not in conflict with existing legislation
option_e: Custom is incorporated if it has been practiced for at least a century
option_f: Custom is incorporated if it is ratified by a national referendum
option_g: Custom is only incorporated if it is universally practiced throughout England
option_h: Custom must first be recognised by Parliament before the courts can bring it into the domestic sphere
option_i: Custom is incorporated if it is not in conflict with the common law
option_j: Custom is incorporated with the passing of implementing legislation

question_text: One afternoon, a pilot was flying a small airplane when it suddenly ran out of gas. As he was coming in for an emergency landing, the plane crossed into a neighboring state at a very low altitude. At this time, a 9-year-old boy was walking to school when he was struck and injured by an object, which may have fallen from the plane. In federal court, a negligence suit was brought against the pilot by the father of the boy for his son. Accompanied by his father, the boy had visited an attorney for preliminary discussions regarding the case. However, the father did not retain the attorney to represent his son in the lawsuit. Instead, the father hired another lawyer to handle the case. At trial, the pilot's attorney calls the consulting attorney to testify what the boy had said to him regarding his physical condition during the consultation that the attorney had had with the boy and his father. The attorney's testimony is
option_a: admissible, because the father did not retain the consulting attorney, thus nullifying any privilege.
option_b: inadmissible, because it was a statement of physical condition not made for the purpose of obtaining medical treatment.
option_c: inadmissible, because the attorney-client privilege prevents such a breach of confidential communications.
option_d: inadmissible, because the attorney-client privilege is maintained even if a different attorney is hired.
option_e: admissible, because the lawsuit makes all previous communications related to the case public.
option_f: admissible, because the boy was not the attorney's client, only the father was.
option_g: admissible, because there is no privilege of confidentiality when a person other than the client is present at the attorney-client consultation.
option_h: admissible, because the attorney-client privilege was waived by the filing of the lawsuit.
option_i: inadmissible, because the consultation was preliminary and does not constitute a formal attorney-client relationship.
option_j: inadmissible, because the boy is a minor and cannot legally waive his rights to attorney-client privilege.

question_text: Bill, a single man, owned pasture land in Deerwoods, Florida, which he leased to a tenant. He also owned a condominium in Miami, which he held for investment. In his will, he devised the pasture land to his son Tommy and the condominium to his daughter Julie. All other assets would pass equally to Tommy and Julie. Bill met Kathy and married her after she executed a valid prenuptial agreement relinquishing all rights she might otherwise enjoy by marrying Bill. On their Miami honeymoon they drove by the condominium and Kathy declared she'd love to live there. Bill was so happy with Kathy that after the honeymoon he signed and delivered to Kathy a deed conveying the condominium to himself and Kathy as an estate by the entirety and made plans to live in the condominium as soon as the tenant vacated. Bill died the next day. How are the foregoing assets distributed?
option_a: Kathy gets the condominium, but because Bill had originally indicated his intent to devise equally to his children, Tommy and Julie will split the remaining estate.
option_b: Julie receives the condominium due to her father's original will, Tommy gets the pasture land, and the rest of the estate is split between Tommy and Julie.
option_c: Kathy gets the condominium due to the deed, Tommy gets the pasture land, and the rest of the estate is split evenly between Tommy, Julie, and Kathy.
option_d: Kathy, as the surviving spouse, gets the entire estate regardless of the prenuptial agreement.
option_e: Tommy and Julie split all assets equally, as the prenuptial agreement nullifies any changes made after the marriage.
option_f: Regardless of the prenuptial agreement, Kathy is a pretermitted spouse. Since Bill leaves surviving lineal descendants who are not Kathy's, Kathy receives 50% of the estate, Tommy gets the pasture land, and Tommy and Julie split the residue of the estate.
option_g: Kathy gets the condominium regardless of the prenuptial agreement, Tommy takes the pasture land and Tommy and Julie split the rest of the estate.
option_h: Tommy receives the pasture land, Julie gets the condominium, and the rest of the estate is split between Tommy and Julie, as Kathy signed a prenuptial agreement.
option_i: Tommy gets the pasture land, Kathy gets the condominium due to her husband's last wish, and the remaining estate is split between Tommy, Julie, and Kathy.
option_j: Due to Kathy's prenuptial agreement, Tommy receives the pasture land, Julie gets the condominium and Tommy and Julie split the rest of the estate.

question_text: An owner operated a successful retail business. He decided he wanted to retire and listed his business for sale. A buyer saw the listing and visited the retail business. The buyer told the owner that she was very interested in buying the business, but that she needed to get a loan from the bank to finance the purchase. The parties drafted a contract, signed by both parties, which stated that the buyer agreed to purchase the retail business from the owner for $250,000. The written agreement contained a provision wherein the sale would not take effect "unless the buyer is successful in her bid to obtain a loan from a bank to finance the purchase of the business. " Subsequently, the buyer made no effort to attempt to obtain a loan from a bank so that the sale could be finalized. After several months, the owner filed suit against the buyer for breach of contract. Which of the following will be the owner's strongest argument in support of his action against the buyer for breach of contract?
option_a: The buyer's lack of effort in obtaining a loan is a breach of good faith and fair dealing.
option_b: The buyer was under a duty to inform the owner of her decision not to seek a loan.
option_c: Although obtaining a loan from a bank was a condition to the buyer's duty to buy the business, the condition should be excused because its non-occurrence would cause a forfeiture to the owner.
option_d: The owner suffered financial loss due to the buyer's inaction in seeking a loan.
option_e: The buyer breached an implied promise to make a reasonable effort to obtain a loan from a bank.
option_f: The obtaining of a loan from a bank was not worded as a "condition" to the buyer's duty to buy the business.
option_g: Although obtaining a loan from a bank was a condition to the buyer's duty to buy the business, it should be stricken from the contract because it is an unconscionable term.
option_h: The buyer did not explicitly state in the contract that she would not seek a loan.

question_text: In which of the following situations would the defendant's intoxication NOT be able to negate his criminal culpability?
option_a: A defendant had been drinking liquor all evening at a bar with three of his buddies. An undercover detective overheard the defendant and his buddies plot to rob the bar after closing hours. When the defendant attempted to draw a gun from his coat, he was quickly disarmed and placed under arrest by the detective. The defendant is charged with the crime of conspiracy to commit robbery.
option_b: After consuming a large amount of alcohol at a party, a defendant decided to set a neighbor's car on fire out of spite. He was seen by witnesses and later arrested and charged with arson.
option_c: A defendant had been drinking at a local pub and got into a heated argument with another patron. The argument escalated, and the defendant pulled out a pocket knife and stabbed the other patron. The defendant is arrested and charged with attempted murder.
option_d: A defendant had consumed a fifth of bourbon. Later that same day, he approached a victim and, brandishing a knife, told her to accompany him or he would stab her. He led the victim to his car and then ordered her to disrobe. As the victim was removing her pantyhose, she kicked the defendant in the head, temporarily dazing him. The victim then safely ran from the car. The defendant is arrested and charged with the crime of assault with the intent to commit rape.
option_e: A defendant spent the evening drinking at a bar. On his way home, he broke into a house, intending to steal valuables. However, he fell asleep on the couch before he could steal anything. He was discovered by the homeowners and arrested and charged with attempted robbery.
option_f: At his law school graduation party, a defendant drank two six-packs of beer. Around midnight, the defendant was approached by a girl, who asked him to drive her home. Although the girl was only 15 years old, she had the appearance of a woman in her mid-to-late twenties. The defendant, who had had his eye on the girl all night, quickly agreed, and he showed her the way to his car. Once inside, they engaged in sexual intercourse. The age of consent in this jurisdiction is 17 years old for females. The defendant is subsequently arrested and charged with statutory rape.
option_g: A defendant was drinking at a bar when he noticed a woman leave her purse unattended. He stole the purse and was later arrested and charged with theft.
option_h: A defendant attended a wedding reception at a hotel, where he drank several vodka daiquiris. Following the reception, the defendant engaged in a violent argument with the hotel's parking lot attendant. The defendant took a tire iron from his car and threw it at the attendant. The tire iron missed the attendant and hit a victim as he was entering the hotel. The defendant is arrested and charged with assault with the intent to commit battery.
option_i: A defendant had been drinking heavily at a friend's house. Later that evening, he decided to drive home despite being significantly over the legal blood alcohol limit. On the way, he ran a red light and hit a pedestrian who was crossing the street. The defendant is arrested and charged with vehicular manslaughter.
option_j: After a night of heavy drinking, a defendant broke into a jewelry store and stole several expensive items. He was caught on the store's surveillance cameras and later arrested and charged with burglary.

question_text: Believing she was pregnant, a woman went to see a doctor, a licensed gynecologist, because she wanted an abortion. The doctor examined her and determined that she was not pregnant, but decided that he would tell her she was, in order to earn his $500 fee. After receiving the $500, the doctor proceeded to have the woman admitted to a hospital by falsely informing the hospital authorities that she had a benign tumor on her uterus, which he was going to remove. He performed all the surgical procedures appropriate for an abortion under adequate hygienic conditions, but the woman began hemorrhaging and died. The doctor should be found guilty of
option_f: murder, manslaughter, and larceny by trick.
option_i: battery, manslaughter, and larceny by trick.

question_text: After a wife found out about her husband's infidelity, she decided to have him killed. The defendant approached a hit man to solicit his assistance in carrying out the planned murder. Although the wife believed that he was a hit man, he was, in fact, an undercover agent. The wife told the undercovr agent she would pay him $20,000 if he accepted the job and an additional $20,000 after the killing was completed. Upon hearing the wife's proposal, the undercover agent told the wife he would kill her husband. However, the undercover agent secretly intended not to go through with the plan, and he merely feigned agreement because he wished to trap the wife. The wife told the undercover agent that she would deliver the first payment the next day. However, before making the initial payment, the wife learned that the hit man was really an undercover agent. Fearful that she might be prosecuted for planning her husband's murder, the wife contacted the police and renounced her participation in the criminal endeavor. The defendant will most likely be found
option_a: not guilty of either solicitation or conspiracy to commit murder.
option_b: not guilty due to lack of intent.
option_d: guilty of solicitation and conspiracy to commit murder.
option_e: not guilty due to renouncing her participation.
option_g: guilty of conspiracy to commit murder and attempted murder.
option_h: not guilty of conspiracy to commit murder, but guilty of solicitation.
option_i: guilty of attempted murder and solicitation.
option_j: guilty of conspiracy to commit murder.

question_text: A truck driver from State A and a bus driver from State B were involved in a collision in State B that injured the truck driver. The truck driver filed a federal diversity action in State B based on negligence, seeking $100,000 in damages from the bus driver. What law of negligence should the court apply?
option_a: The court should apply the federal common law of negligence.
option_b: The court should consider the negligence law of both State A and State B and apply the law that the court believes most appropriately governs negligence in this action.
option_c: The court should apply the negligence law of the state with the highest population.
option_d: The court should apply international negligence law.
option_e: The court should apply the negligence law of the state where the truck driver's company is headquartered.
option_f: The court should determine which state's negligence law a state court in State B would apply and apply that law in this action.
option_g: The court should apply the negligence law of the state where the bus driver resides.
option_h: The court should apply the federal law of the state where the accident occurred.
option_i: The court should apply the negligence law of State B, the location of the accident.
option_j: The court should apply the negligence law of State A, the truck driver's state of citizenship.

question_text: A university student, a citizen of State A, believes that her university violated federal law when it eliminated funding for the women's varsity volleyball team. She has sued the university, a nonprofit corporation located and incorporated in State B, and the university's athletic director, a citizen of State B, in federal court in State B. What is the best method of serving the defendants?
option_a: Service by publication in a newspaper of general circulation in State B.
option_b: Service as required by State B's rules of civil procedure.
option_c: Service by emailing copies of the summons and complaint to the athletic director and the university president.
option_d: Service by faxing copies of the summons and complaint to the athletic director and the university president.
option_e: Service by the student herself on the athletic director and the university president.
option_f: Service by a process server's leaving copies of the summons and complaint with copies of the summons and complaint with secretaries in the respective offices of the athletic director and the university president.
option_g: Service by the student's attorney on the athletic director and the university president.
option_h: Service by sending copies of the summons and complaint through the university's internal mail system.
option_i: Service by leaving copies of the summons and complaint at the university's main entrance.

question_text: A debtor owed a creditor $750 on an old debt. On July 1, the debt was barred by the statute of limitations. On August 1, the debtor ran into the creditor at a party and overheard him telling mutual friends that the debtor "is a deadbeat who weiches on his debts. " Feeling pangs of guilt, the debtor approached the creditor and orally agreed to pay him the $750 debt on September 1. The debtor refused to pay the creditor the $750 as promised on September 1St. If the creditor sues the debtor to recover the $750 debt, which would provide the strongest grounds that the debtor's oral promise was unenforceable?
option_a: There was no mutuality of obligation.
option_b: The creditor's claim was based on hearsay.
option_c: The promise was made out of guilt, not legal obligation.
option_d: It was violative of the statute of frauds.
option_e: The debtor was under duress when he made the promise.
option_f: The debtor had no legal capacity to enter into the agreement.
option_g: The debt was already barred by the statute of limitations.
option_h: The promise was not made in a formal legal setting.
option_i: It was not supported by new consideration.
option_j: The debtor did not provide a written agreement for the promise.

question_text: There may be a substantial time period between contract formation and final completion of performance. Concerning these executory contracts, which of the following is the least correct?
option_a: A fully executory contract is when both parties have completed their performance.
option_b: A partially executed contract means that both parties have begun but not completed performance.
option_c: An executory contract is when one party has performed and the other party has not yet begun performance.
option_d: An executed contract exists when both parties have fully performed and no obligations remain.
option_e: A partially executed contract means that at least one party has begun performance.
option_f: A partially executory contract is when one party has begun performance and the other party has completed performance.
option_g: A fully executed contract exists when neither party has any remaining obligations.
option_h: A partially executed contract means that one party has completed performance while the other party has only promised.
option_i: A wholly executory contract is where only promises have been exchanged and there has been no performance by either party.
option_j: An executory contract is when both parties have yet to begin performance.

question_text: A woman was hit in the right eye by a shard of glass when her car was rear-ended by an industrial truck. She was hospitalized, lost her eye, and had several operations over several years. She could not return to work as an apprentice ballet dancer due to loss of coordination, constant headaches, and severe disfigurement. She is considered totally disabled. She had been training for the ballet for many years and, at the trial against the tortfeasor, she proved that she was considered a bright and uncoming student. The defendant claimed that this was still too speculative whether she would have been successful. The trial court disagreed and allowed evidence on the lost earning capacity of a ballet dancer. The jury awarded $3 million. Will the appellate court likely reverse the verdict due to the lost earning capacity issue?
option_a: Yes, because the damages awarded are excessive and not substantiated by concrete proof of earning capacity.
option_b: Yes, because the plaintiff failed to prove that she would have definitely become a successful professional ballet dancer.
option_c: Yes, because the defendant has the right to challenge the amount of the award given by the jury.
option_d: Yes, the evidence did not guarantee or assure anyone that she really would have been a successful professional ballet dancer.
option_e: No, the jury's award is discretionary and inviolate, and it may not be disturbed with respect to personal injury damages.
option_f: Yes, she can only collect for lost work that she held prior to the accident; her future lost earning capacity could be based on an apprentice ballet dancer.
option_g: No, because some imprecision in the evidence regarding loss of earning capacity is to be expected and is not grounds for excluding the evidence.

question_text: In 1996, a developer purchased a 100-acre tract located in a northern county in a state. Shortly thereafter, the developer prepared a subdivision plan that created 100 one-acre residential building lots on this tract. In 1997, the subdivision plan was recorded with the county recorder's office. During the next few years, the developer sold 60 residential lots to individual purchasers. Each deed specified that every lot designated on the subdivision plan was to be recorded in the county recorder's office. Each deed also provided the following:"No house trailer or mobile home shall be built or maintained on any lot within the subdivision. "In 2003, the developer conveyed the remaining 40 lots to a builder by deed that included language identical to that contained in the first 60 deeds. This deed from the developer to the builder was recorded. By 2008, the builder had sold all of the 40 lots. Each of these deeds identified each lot as being a part of the subdivision, but did not include the clause relating to mobile homes. On January 30, 2009, a buyer, who had purchased one of the residential lots from the builder, placed a mobile home on his property. Which of the following statements is LEAST accurate with respect to the buyer's deed?
option_a: The buyer has no obligation to remove the mobile home.
option_b: All subsequent grantees of the builder would be in privity of contract.
option_c: The deed from the builder to the buyer did not include the covenant prohibiting mobile homes.
option_d: The covenant prohibiting mobile homes ran with the land as far as the builder, but not as far as the buyer.
option_e: All subsequent grantees of the builder would be in privity of estate.
option_f: The buyer should have had constructive notice of the restriction against mobile homes.
option_g: The buyer should have had actual notice of the restriction against mobile homes.
option_h: The covenant prohibiting mobile homes could be enforced by any subdivision lot owner.
option_i: The covenant prohibiting mobile homes was not recorded with the county recorder's office.
option_j: The covenant prohibiting mobile homes could only be enforced by the original developer.

question_text: A collector owned a rare 16th-century tapestry. The collector contracted in writing to sell the tapestry to a tapestry dealer for $100,000. The contract stipulated that delivery would be "F. O. B. at the dealer's shop," with payment to be made one week after tender. When the dealer received the tapestry, he noticed that it had been damaged in transit. The dealer immediately contacted the collector and notified him that he was rejecting the tapestry because it had ripped apart and was becoming unwoven. The collector told the dealer that he would get back to him with re-shipping instructions. The collector did not make any further contact with the dealer. After four weeks, the dealer then sold the tapestry to a buyer for $120,000. If the collector sues the dealer for damages, the collector should recover
option_a: $20,000, which covers the difference between the contract price and the sale price.
option_b: $110,000, which includes a 10% penalty on the resale.
option_e: Nothing, because the dealer acted within his rights after the collector failed to respond.
option_f: $100,000, because the dealer is entitled to the contract price.
option_g: $20,000, for the loss of potential profit due to the damaged tapestry.
option_i: $108,000, because the dealer is entitled to a reasonable sum not exceeding 10% on the resale.
option_j: $130,000, including the contract price and a penalty for unauthorized resale.

question_text: A man worked as a clerk in a gourmet coffee shop. His friend often stopped by the shop to chat with the man while the man was working. One afternoon, while the friend was visiting the shop, the man needed to use the bathroom. There were no customers in the shop at that moment, so the man asked the friend to watch the store for him while he went in the back to use the bathroom. The friend agreed to do so. While the man was in the bathroom, a customer came into the store. She wanted to purchase some freshly ground coffee. The shop had a grinding machine that customers could use. The customer selected a bag of coffee beans and then took it to the grinding machine. She was unsure how to start the machine, so she asked the friend for help. The friend tried to show the customer how to use the machine, but he was not familiar with how it worked, and he gave the customer erroneous instructions. The customer tried to follow the friend's instructions, but this led to her getting her finger pinched and cut by the machine. If the customer asserts a claim against the coffee shop company for her finger injury, the customer will most likely
option_a: prevail, because the company should have had a warning sign about the dangers of the machine.
option_b: not prevail, because the company is not liable for the friend's negligence.
option_c: prevail, because the company would be vicariously liable for the man's unauthorized actions.
option_d: not prevail, because the customer should have waited for an employee to operate the machine.
option_e: not prevail, because the friend was acting as a Good Samaritan.
option_f: not prevail, because the man acted outside the scope of employment by entrusting the supervision of the shop to his friend.
option_g: prevail, because the company failed to properly train the man on how to operate the machine.
option_h: not prevail, because the friend was not an employee of the coffee shop.
option_i: prevail, because the company failed to ensure that the man was never left alone in the shop.
option_j: prevail, because the friend's negligence would be imputed to the company.

question_text: A patient domiciled in State A sued a surgeon domiciled in State B in a federal court in State A, alleging claims for malpractice. The surgeon moved to dismiss the action for lack of personal jurisdiction. The court denied the motion and set discovery cutoff and trial dates. The surgeon has appealed the denial of the motion. Should the appellate court hear the merits of the surgeon's appeal?
option_a: No, because the district court's decision on jurisdiction is final.
option_b: No, because the trial dates have already been set.
option_c: Yes, because the federal court in State A may have been biased.
option_d: Yes, because the surgeon's livelihood is at risk.
option_e: No, because the patient's right to sue is protected by the constitution.
option_f: No, because the appellate court lacks jurisdiction over the appeal.
option_g: No, because the surgeon should have filed a motion in State B.
option_h: Yes, because a contrary appellate decision could terminate the action.
option_i: Yes, because the surgeon has the right to appeal any court decision.
option_j: Yes, because the surgeon's personal- jurisdiction challenge raises a constitutional question.

question_text: An entrepreneur from State A decided to sell hot sauce to the public, labeling it ÒBest Hot Sauce.Ó A company incorporated in State B and headquartered in State C sued the entrepreneur in federal court in State C. The complaint sought $50,000 in damages and alleged that the entrepreneur's use of the name ÒBest Hot SauceÓ infringed the company's federal trademark. The entrepreneur filed an answer denying the allegations, and the parties began discovery. Six months later, the entrepreneur moved to dismiss for lack of subject-matter jurisdiction. Should the court grant the entrepreneur's motion?
option_a: Yes, because although there is diversity, the amount in controversy is not satisfied.
option_b: No, because the entrepreneur waived the right to challenge subject-matter jurisdiction by not raising the issue initially by motion or in the answer.
option_c: No, because the company's headquarters are in the same state as the court.
option_d: Yes, because the complaint is about a product, not a legal matter.
option_e: No, because the entrepreneur's product is being sold to the public.
option_f: No, because the entrepreneur has been participating in the case for six months.
option_g: Yes, because the entrepreneur is not from the same state as the company.
option_h: Yes, because the entrepreneur is not a resident of the state where the court is located.
option_i: No, because the company's claim arises under federal law.
option_j: Yes, because although the claim arises under federal law, the amount in controversy is not satisfied.

question_text: While relaxing at poolside one Sunday afternoon, a homeowner was struck by a golf ball driven by a 14-year-old boy who was playing the ninth hole at the local golf course. The fairway for the ninth hole was 65 feet wide and 437 yards long, with a dogleg in an easterly direction. Between the fairway and the homeowner's property was a "rough," containing brush and low-lying trees. The boy had hit a towering shot down the middle of the fairway that deflected off a tree and struck the homeowner in the head. The homeowner brought suit against the boy for his injuries suffered when he was hit by the golf ball. At trial, the boy offered uncontested evidence that golf balls from the golf course regularly traversed onto the homeowner's property two to three times a day. Which of the following statements is most accurate regarding the boy's liability for trespass?
option_a: The boy is not liable, because he did not intentionally cause the golf ball to traverse onto the plaintiff's property.
option_b: Because the golf ball did not substantially interfere with the plaintiff's use and enjoyment of his land, the boy would not be held liable.
option_c: The boy is not liable because the golf course should have better protective measures to prevent golf balls from entering neighboring properties.
option_d: The boy would be liable only if he was aware that his golf balls regularly traversed onto the homeowner's property.
option_e: The boy is liable because the frequency of golf balls entering the homeowner's property suggests a lack of care in his play.
option_f: The boy would be liable for the unpermitted intrusion of the golf ball onto the plaintiff's property.
option_g: The boy is liable because he was negligent in hitting the golf ball in the direction of the homeowner's property.

question_text: A man wearing a jacket and jeans walked along a city street at night in a high crime area known for drug trafficking and gun violence. He saw two police officers walking toward him. The officers were checking on a 911 call from a resident who observed a man in jeans and a jacket apparently selling drugs to people on the street. When the police first saw him, he was walking alone, in the area that the woman identified. As they approached, he suddenly crossed the street by jaywalking. They followed, and approaching from behind, asked him to stop. He started walking faster, away from them. The officers saw what appeared to be a heavy object bulging in his pocket as he moved. The officers, with over 10 years of foot patrol experience, stepped up and stopped him. They patted him down and found a concealed, unlicensed handgun in the jacket pocket. They then made a full search and found 50 packets of heroin. He filed a motion to suppress the gun and drug evidence. Will the court likely suppress the evidence?
option_a: No, because the accumulation of factors all added up to reasonable suspicion for a stop and articulable suspicion for a frisk.
option_b: No, because the man's suspicious behavior and the bulge in his pocket gave the police probable cause to search him.
option_c: Yes, because the police lacked reasonable suspicion to stop the man simply because he crossed the street and walked faster away from them.
option_d: Yes, because the police didn't have a warrant to search the man.
option_e: Yes, because the heavy object in the man's pocket could have been anything and didn't necessarily indicate criminal activity.
option_f: No, because the police had probable cause to make an arrest when the man began to walk away from them and walk even faster as they followed him.
option_g: No, because the police had witnessed a crime, jaywalking, which gave them the authority to stop the man.
option_h: Yes, because they didn't know if this was the same man, they observed nothing illegal except jaywalking, and he had a right to avoid them under the circumstances.
option_i: Yes, because the police had no evidence that the man was the same one the resident had reported.
option_j: No, because the man matched the description of the suspected drug dealer and was in a high crime area.

question_text: A defendant charged with first-degree murder shall be furnished with a list containing names and addresses of all prospective jurors
option_c: upon request and showing of good cause.
option_f: only if the jurors give consent.
option_g: at the discretion of the judge.
option_j: only if the defendant can prove it's necessary for their defense.

question_text: A father and a son were indicted and charged with bank robbery. They allegedly participated together in robbing a bank in their hometown. After the son was arrested and given Miranda warnings, he confessed and admitted that he and his father took part in the bank robbery. The prosecution decided to charge the father and son in the same indictment and prosecute them together in a single trial. At trial, the son took the stand and recanted his earlier confession by denying participation in the robbery. The prosecution now seeks to introduce into evidence the son's earlier confession that was videotaped by the police. The son and the father both object to its admissibility. The confession should be
option_a: admitted against the father only, because the son recanted his confession.
option_b: not admitted against the son, but admitted against the father as it implicates him in the crime.
option_c: admitted against both the son and the father, because the son implicated the father in the crime.
option_d: not admitted against the son or the father, because the son was under duress when he confessed.
option_e: admitted against the son only, because it is his confession.
option_f: admitted against both the son and the father, because they were jointly charged.
option_g: not admitted against the son or the father, because the confession is hearsay not within any recognized exception.
option_h: not admitted against the son or the father, because the son recanted the confession.
option_i: admitted against both the son and the father, because they were engaged in a conspiracy.

question_text: One evening, a defendant set fire to a homeowner's occupied house. As a result of the blaze, the homeowner's daughter was killed. The defendant was charged with felony murder on the first count and arson on the second count of the two-count indictment. The jury found the defendant guilty on the first count, but returned a not guilty verdict on the second. The defendant's attorney's motion to set aside the guilty verdict on the felony murder charge will most likely be
option_a: granted, because the defendant's right to a fair trial was violated by the inconsistent verdicts.
option_b: denied, because the jury's decision is final and cannot be overturned.
option_c: denied, because the defendant's proper remedy is to seek an appellate review for a non-constitutional error.
option_d: denied, because the verdicts do not amount to a reversible error.
option_e: granted, because the guilty verdict is plain error that adversely affects the defendant's constitutional rights.
option_f: granted, because the verdicts contradict each other, raising reasonable doubt.
option_g: granted, because the verdicts are legally inconsistent and should lead to an acquittal of both charges.
option_h: denied, because the jury found sufficient evidence to convict on the first count, regardless of the second count outcome.
option_i: granted, because the defendant was not directly responsible for the death.

question_text: A state enacted a statute that authorized the payment of state funds to any private institution of higher learning within the state that meets certain minimum criteria and refrains from awarding only seminarian or theological degrees. The aid is in the form of annual subsidies to qualified institutions. The grants are non-categorical but may not, under an added provision, be utilized by the institutions for sectarian purposes. A state agency determines the eligibility of applicant institutions. Eight state taxpayers, all avowed atheists, bring suit in federal court to enjoin the payments of subsidies under the statute to all church-affiliated institutions in the state. The federal district court will most likely
option_a: uphold the validity of the statute as a valid exercise of the state's power to subsidize education.
option_b: dismiss the action for lack of standing.
option_c: invalidate the statute on the grounds of separation of church and state.
option_d: invalidate the statute as a valid exercise of the state's power to subsidize education.
option_e: uphold the validity of the statute but prohibit the subsidies to church-affiliated institutions.
option_f: invalidate the statute as violative of the establishment clause.
option_g: dismiss the action because the plaintiffs are not directly affected by the statute.
option_h: uphold the validity of the statute as violative of the establishment clause.
option_i: uphold the validity of the statute as nonviolative of the establishment clause.
option_j: dismiss the action on the basis that the statute does not promote a specific religion.

question_text: A plaintiff was the varsity basketball coach of the boys' team at a high school. After the second game of the season, the plaintiff was suddenly dismissed as the coach for undisclosed reasons. After the dismissal was made public, the local newspaper ran an article discussing the plaintiff's tenure as coach. The article, which was written by the sports editor, stated that "The plaintiff was a bad role model and morally unfit for the coaching job. "The plaintiff brought suit against the newspaper for defamation. At trial, the plaintiff called a player on the basketball team to testify that "the plaintiff was a good role model and respected by the players. " In presenting its evidence, the newspaper called a witness to testify. He proposes to testify that he saw the plaintiff give beer to players on three occasions after victories. Upon objection by the plaintiff's attorney, witness's testimony is
option_a: admissible, as it provides a motive for the plaintiff's dismissal.
option_b: inadmissible to prove character, but admissible for the purpose of showing intent or common scheme.
option_e: inadmissible, as it is not relevant to the case.
